Aluno
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

João Guilherme da Costa e Silva

Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

B2 (Intermediário-Superior) São Paulo, SP Engenheiro de Sinalização — Motiva 60 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Diagnostic + Reactivation -- Who Is João Guilherme in English?

Introduce yourself and describe your role on a kick-off call with an international supplier. Key words: signaling system, commissioning, supplier, milestone, deliverable, stakeholder, compliance, inspection, specification, scope, deadline, to oversee. Structures: present simple (current role) vs present perfect (experience, for/since) vs present perfect continuous (what is still going on) vs past simple (finished time).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to every term and read the example sentence. These are the twelve words you already handle at Motiva every single day.

Signaling system -- the equipment that controls train movement and keeps trains safely apart
"I design the signaling system for two metro lines."
Commissioning -- the final stage where you test and prove that a system works before it goes live
"The commissioning of Line 5 starts in September."
Supplier -- the company that provides the equipment or the service
"Our supplier is based in Germany."
Milestone -- a key date in the project that marks the end of a stage
"We missed the milestone by three weeks."
Deliverable -- a document or a product that the project must hand over
"The test report is the next deliverable."
Stakeholder -- anyone with an interest in the outcome of the project
"Every stakeholder wants a realistic schedule."
Compliance -- doing exactly what a standard or a contract requires
"Compliance with the European standard is mandatory."
Inspection -- a formal check to confirm quality before you accept the work
"The inspection takes place at the factory."
Specification -- the written document that defines what the equipment must do
"The specification says the response time is two seconds."
Scope -- everything the project includes, and nothing more
"That request is outside the scope of the contract."
Deadline -- the date by which the work must be finished
"The supplier missed the deadline again."
To oversee -- to be in charge of a process and supervise it
"I oversee the commissioning of the signaling system."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match every term with the right definition.

Signaling system
Commissioning
Supplier
Milestone
Deliverable
Stakeholder
Compliance
Inspection
Specification
Scope
Deadline
To oversee

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

João Guilherme is a signaling engineer at Motiva. He oversees the commissioning of the signaling system on two metro lines, and he runs a weekly status meeting with the suppliers. "I have worked in railway signaling for eight years," he says, "and I have been working with German, French and Chinese suppliers since 2019." In 2023, he spent two months in San Francisco, where he commissioned equipment in the field. Today his week is a schedule: he reviews the specification, he prepares the factory inspection, and he checks compliance with the European standard, because compliance is mandatory. The next milestone is three weeks away and the test report, the first deliverable, has not arrived yet. The team has been waiting for the revised specification since March, the deadline is fixed, and the extra training the client asked for is outside the scope. Every stakeholder wants a schedule they can trust.

1. Why do we say "I have worked in railway signaling for eight years" and not "I work in railway signaling for eight years"?
A It started in the past and it is still true today — present perfect with for.
B The action is over and it has no connection with the present.
C It is a routine he repeats every week.
2. "In 2023, he spent two months in San Francisco" takes the past simple because:
A The experience is still going on today.
B It is a closed period, inside a finished time frame (in 2023).
C It is happening right now.
3. "The team has been waiting for the revised specification since March." What does this sentence tell you?
A The team waited in March and the document has already arrived.
B The wait started in March and it is STILL going on — the document has not arrived.
C The team will start waiting in March.
4. Which sentence about his current role is correct?
A "He is overseeing the commissioning since eight years."
B "He oversees the commissioning and runs a weekly status meeting."
C "He has overseen the commissioning in 2023."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Perfect vs Present Perfect Continuous vs Past Simple

GRAMMAR

How to tell your career story with the right tense — the very thing every interviewer and every supplier tests in the first two minutes of a call.

FormUseExample
Present Simple
I / you / we + verb (he/she + s)		Current role and routine.I oversee the commissioning.
Present Perfect
have / has + past participle		Experience and duration up to now (for / since).I have worked in signaling for eight years.
Present Perfect Continuous
have been + verb-ing		An activity that started in the past and is still going on right now.I have been working with German suppliers since 2019.
Past Simple
verb + -ed / irregular		Finished action, finished time frame (in 2023).I spent two months in San Francisco in 2023.
Negative (present perfect)have / has + not + past participle. In speech, contracted: haven't.We haven't received the specification.
QuestionHow long + have + subject + past participle?How long have you been in signaling?
for vs sincefor + a period of time (for eight years) · since + a starting point (since 2019)

Watch out — the classic Brazilian trap: your first language expresses duration up to now with a PRESENT tense verb, so "I am an engineer for eight years" and "I work with Germans since 2019" feel natural. Both are wrong. In English, duration up to now never takes the present simple: say "I have been an engineer for eight years" (in speech, I've been) and "I have been working with German suppliers since 2019".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete every sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I in railway signaling for eight years."
"In my current role, I the commissioning of the signaling system."
"I with German suppliers since 2019."
"In 2023, I two months in San Francisco."
"I have worked in railway signaling eight years."
"I have been working with German suppliers 2019."

Stage 2: Put the Kick-Off Call in Order

Order

Put the steps of a kick-off call with an international supplier in the right order.

?Describe your current role: what you oversee and which lines you work on.
?Agree on the next step and confirm who sends what, and by when.
?Thank the supplier for joining and confirm everyone can hear you.
?Raise the delay on the specification and ask about the factory inspection.
?Introduce yourself: your name, your company and how long you have been in signaling.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the model, then record yourself saying it. These five lines open almost any meeting or job interview.

I'm a signaling engineer at Motiva, and I oversee the commissioning.
I've worked in railway signaling for eight years.
I've been working with German suppliers since 2019.
My main concern is the deadline for the factory inspection.
Sorry, could you rephrase that? I want to make sure I understand.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the strongest answer for these real moments in a meeting or a job interview.

The supplier's project manager asks: "So, tell me about your role." You answer:
A "I do the signaling things in the company."
B "I'm a signaling engineer at Motiva, and I oversee the commissioning of the signaling system on two metro lines."
C "I'm overseeing the commissioning since eight years."
She asks: "How long have you been in signaling?" The best answer is:
A "I am in signaling for eight years."
B "I've worked in railway signaling for eight years."
C "I work in railway signaling since eight years."
You want to mention a finished period of your career. Which is correct?
A "In 2023, I spent two months in San Francisco."
B "In 2023, I have spent two months in San Francisco."
C "In 2023, I am spending two months in San Francisco."
The specification has not arrived and you are still waiting. You say:
A "We wait for the specification since March."
B "We've been waiting for the specification since March."
C "We waited for the specification since March."
You didn't understand a question and you need a moment. The most professional move is:
A Answer something vague and hope nobody notices.
B "Sorry, could you rephrase that? I want to make sure I understand."
C Apologize for your English and switch to Portuguese.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. There is no right or wrong version — speak for 90 seconds, off the cuff. This recording is your BASELINE: we will play it back in Lesson 12 and in Lesson 24.

You are starting a video call with a new international supplier. Introduce yourself: your current role at Motiva (present simple), how long you have been in railway signaling (present perfect, for/since), who you have been working with since 2019 (present perfect continuous), and what you did in San Francisco in 2023 (past simple). Finish with your main concern on the project. Take your time and don't read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1I'm a signaling engineer at Motiva, and I oversee the commissioning.
2I've worked in railway signaling for eight years.
3I've been working with German suppliers since 2019.
4My main concern is the deadline for the factory inspection.
5Sorry, could you rephrase that? I want to make sure I understand.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

Reactivating Your Professional Voice -- Roles, Responsibilities and Ongoing Projects

Describe your role at Motiva and run a project retrospective without blaming anyone. Key words: to specialize in, to coordinate, to liaise with, to carry out, to look into, to come up with, interface document, subcontractor, procurement, handover, workstream, counterpart, lead time, tip of the iceberg. Structure: third conditional (If + had + past participle, would have + past participle) + the patterns "I'm responsible for / My role involves / We're currently in the process of".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 1 you named the THINGS on a project. Here you name the ACTIONS: what you do, who you do it with, and what you deliver. Listen to each term and read the example sentence.

To specialize in -- to focus your career on one specific area of expertise
"I specialize in CBTC signaling for metro lines."
To coordinate -- to organize people and activities so that they work together
"I coordinate the design team and the field team."
To liaise with -- to be the bridge between two sides and exchange information with them
"I liaise with the German and Spanish suppliers every week."
To carry out -- to perform and complete a task, a test or an inspection
"We carry out the inspection at the supplier's factory."
To look into -- to investigate a problem before you answer or decide
"I will look into the delay and come back to you tomorrow."
To come up with -- to propose a solution or produce an idea
"The team came up with a workaround in two days."
Interface document -- the document that defines how two systems connect to each other
"The interface document is signed by both suppliers."
Subcontractor -- a company hired by the main contractor to do one part of the work
"The cabling is done by a local subcontractor."
Procurement -- the process of buying the equipment and the services a project needs
"Procurement placed the order three months late."
Handover -- the formal moment when you transfer a finished system to the client
"The handover to the operator is planned for June."
Workstream -- one parallel line of work inside a bigger project
"I run the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension."
Counterpart -- the person who has the same role as you in the other company
"My counterpart in Madrid signs the interface document."
Lead time -- the time between placing an order and receiving the equipment
"The lead time for the interlocking is nine months."
Tip of the iceberg -- a small visible problem that signals a much bigger one under the surface
"The late drawings are just the tip of the iceberg."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match every term with the right definition.

To specialize in
To coordinate
To liaise with
To carry out
To look into
To come up with
Interface document
Subcontractor
Procurement
Handover
Workstream
Counterpart
Lead time
Tip of the iceberg

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text, then answer the questions below it.

João Guilherme is responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension. He specializes in CBTC, he coordinates the design team and the field team, and he liaises with every supplier on the package. His counterpart at each supplier signs the interface document that defines how the two systems connect. Every quarter he carries out a factory inspection in Europe, and the cabling on site is done by a local subcontractor.

The project is three months late, and the retrospective is honest. If the interface document had arrived in January, the team would have completed the design review before the summer. If procurement had been involved in that review, the order would have been placed in January and the nine-month lead time would not have hurt the schedule. If somebody had looked into the first alarm in March, the team would have come up with a workaround in April.

Today the handover to the operator is still officially planned for June, and nobody believes that date. As João says on the call with the new supplier: "Honestly, the late document is only the tip of the iceberg. One of the main challenges we face is the lead time, and I would rather bring a realistic date than an optimistic one."

1. "If the interface document had arrived in January, the team would have completed the design review before the summer." Did the document arrive in January?
A No. The third conditional describes an IMAGINED past — the condition did not happen, so the result did not happen either.
B Yes. It arrived in January and the design review was closed.
C We still don't know — the sentence is about the future.
2. What is the correct form of the CONDITION in the third conditional?
A If + would have + past participle
B If + had + past participle
C If + past simple
3. "If procurement had been involved earlier, the order would have been placed in January." Why is this sentence more professional than "Procurement forgot the order"?
A Because it is longer and sounds more formal.
B Because it describes the PROCESS that failed, without blaming a person or a company — the language of a retrospective.
C Because it avoids mentioning the delay.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "If they would have signed the document, we would have finished on time."
B "If they had signed the document, we would finished on time."
C "If they had signed the document, we would have finished on time."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Third Conditional

GRAMMAR

How to tell the story of what SHOULD have happened on a project — without blaming anyone.

FormUseExample
Affirmative
If + had + past participle, would have + past participle		An imagined past: the condition did not happen, so the result did not happen either.If the supplier had delivered on time, we would have completed the handover in March.
Negativewouldn't have + past participle. In speech, always contracted.If we had involved procurement earlier, we wouldn't have missed the milestone.
QuestionWhat would have happened if + had + past participle?What would have happened if the order had been placed in January?
In speech (contraction)Both halves contract — this is what you actually hear on a call.If they'd sent the drawings, we'd have caught the error.
Second conditional (contrast)
If + past simple, would + verb		An unreal present or future — not the past.If we had more time, we would run another test tomorrow.
Why it mattersThe third conditional lets you review a missed milestone as a process failure instead of one person's fault. It is the language of a professional retrospective — and what separates a senior engineer from an angry one.

Watch out (classic mistake): the temptation is to put would have in BOTH halves — "if the supplier would have delivered". In English that is WRONG: the condition ALWAYS takes had + past participle ("if the supplier had delivered"). Would have belongs only in the RESULT.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete every sentence with the right form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If the interface document in January, we would have completed the handover on time."
"If the supplier had delivered on time, we the commissioning in March."
"I every supplier on the signaling package."
"We the inspection at the factory before the shipment."
"I am the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension."
"The for the interlocking is nine months."

Stage 2: Put the Project Update in Order

Order

Put the steps of a project update to a new supplier in the right order.

?Explain what went wrong, using the third conditional instead of blaming anyone.
?Name the main challenge ahead and agree on who looks into it, and by when.
?Give the current status: where the project stands right now.
?Describe your role: the workstream you are responsible for and who you coordinate.
?Thank the new supplier for joining and confirm everyone can hear you.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, then record yourself saying it. These five sentences describe your role in any international meeting.

I'm responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension.
My role involves coordinating the design and field teams.
I liaise with every supplier and I carry out the factory inspections.
If the interface document had arrived earlier, we would have completed the handover on time.
Honestly, the late document is only the tip of the iceberg.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the strongest answer for these real moments of a project update.

A new supplier joins the project and asks: "What exactly do you own here?" You answer:
A "I am responsible of the signaling things on Line 5."
B "I'm responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension, and I coordinate the design and field teams."
C "I make the signaling and I see the suppliers."
She asks why the design review is still open. The most professional answer is:
A "If the interface document had arrived in January, we would have closed the review before the summer."
B "The other supplier is incompetent and sent the document three months late."
C "If the interface document would have arrived in January, we would close the review."
You do not have the answer to a technical question during the call. You say:
A "I don't know, sorry."
B "I will look into it and come back to you on Friday with a date."
C "Ask my manager, it is not my area."
The late document is not the only problem. You want to signal a bigger issue without alarming anyone. You say:
A "Everything is fine, do not worry."
B "Honestly, the late document is only the tip of the iceberg."
C "This project is a complete disaster."
Which sentence describes the process of buying the equipment a project needs?
A "Procurement placed the order in April."
B "The handover placed the order in April."
C "The workstream placed the order in April."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. Speak for 90 seconds, with no script and no stopping to fix yourself.

A new international supplier joins your project today. Introduce your role (I'm responsible for... / My role involves...), describe where the project stands (We're currently in the process of...), explain ONE thing that went wrong using the third conditional (If the interface document had arrived in January, we would have...), and name the main challenge ahead (One of the main challenges we face is...). Do not blame anyone -- describe the process.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1I'm responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension.
2My role involves coordinating the design and field teams.
3We're currently in the process of closing the design review.
4If the interface document had arrived earlier, we would have completed the handover on time.
5One of the main challenges we face is the lead time for the interlocking.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

Running and Following Status Meetings -- Reporting, Updating and Pushing Back

Chair a status meeting with a late supplier: report the facts, deduce the cause and push back on the date without breaking the relationship. Key words: non-conformance, punch list, hold point, witness point, inspection test plan (ITP), factory acceptance test (FAT), site acceptance test (SAT), escalation, corrective action, waiver, baseline schedule, to follow up on, to flag up, to push back on. Structure: modal perfects (should have / must have / might have / could not have + past participle) + hedging ("It would appear that...", "One could argue that...", "To some extent I understand your position, however...").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 2 you named the ACTIONS of your role. Here you name the AGENDA of the meeting: what gets inspected, what gets found, and what gets done about it. You already know every one of these concepts from the job — all you are changing is the label. Listen to each term and read the example sentence.

Non-conformance -- a documented failure to meet a requirement of the specification
"We raised a non-conformance on the cable routing."
Punch list -- the list of small open items that must be closed before acceptance
"Three items on the punch list are still open."
Hold point -- a stage where the work must STOP until the inspection has been done
"The cable test is a hold point -- nobody continues without us."
Witness point -- an inspection you may attend, but the work does not stop if you do not
"The insulation test is a witness point, not a hold point."
Inspection test plan (ITP) -- the document that lists every test, who attends it and when
"The ITP defines every hold point on the project."
Factory acceptance test (FAT) -- the test you run at the supplier's factory, before anything is shipped
"The FAT in Berlin is scheduled for October."
Site acceptance test (SAT) -- the same test, repeated on site after the equipment is installed
"The SAT starts two weeks after installation."
Escalation -- taking an unresolved issue to a higher level of management
"If the date slips again, escalation is the only option left."
Corrective action -- the concrete step a supplier takes to fix a non-conformance
"We agreed on a corrective action with a deadline."
Waiver -- a formal written permission to skip one requirement, just once
"They asked for a waiver on the insulation test."
Baseline schedule -- the approved schedule that every delay is measured against
"The revised baseline schedule is three weeks late."
To follow up on -- to check again on something you have already asked for
"I'm following up on the ITP we requested last week."
To flag up -- to raise a problem early, so that nobody is surprised later
"I want to flag up a risk on the delivery date."
To push back on -- to question or resist a request politely, but firmly
"I have to push back on that date -- it is not realistic."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Non-conformance
Punch list
Hold point
Witness point
Inspection test plan (ITP)
Factory acceptance test (FAT)
Site acceptance test (SAT)
Escalation
Corrective action
Waiver
Baseline schedule
To follow up on
To flag up
To push back on

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

It is Monday morning and João Guilherme is chairing the status meeting with CASCO. Four items are open on the punch list. The revised baseline schedule is three weeks overdue, the inspection test plan was submitted with no hold points, a waiver was requested on the insulation test, and a non-conformance is still waiting for a corrective action.

He opens the call. "I would like to follow up on the schedule. It should have reached us on June 10th, and we are now planning the site acceptance test without a date. There must have been a bottleneck on your side." The supplier answers that her team might have been waiting for the interface document — but that document was signed in April, and João has to push back on it. Politely: "To some extent I understand your position, however the document was signed in April."

Then he flags up the second issue. The ITP arrived with witness points only, which means the factory never stops for Motiva. "That should have been discussed with us before the plan was submitted." They agree on a corrective action, a new date, and one last rule: if the date slips again, escalation is the next step. The factory acceptance test in October is already at risk, and nobody wants to discover that in an email.

1. "The revised baseline schedule should have reached us on June 10th." Did the schedule arrive on June 10th?
A No. Should have + past participle describes an obligation that was NOT met — the criticism lands on the ACTION, not on the person.
B Yes. It arrived on time and the item was closed.
C We still do not know — the sentence is about the future.
2. "There must have been a bottleneck on your side." What does must have express here?
A An obligation that was not met.
B A logical DEDUCTION: from the evidence, the speaker is almost certain about what happened.
C A remote, uncertain possibility.
3. Why is "It should have been discussed with us" more professional than "You didn't discuss it with us"?
A Because it is longer and sounds more formal.
B Because it states a fact about the PROCESS without attacking the person — the supplier can agree without losing face, and the meeting ends in a corrective action instead of a fight.
C Because it avoids mentioning the problem.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "The supplier should had submitted the ITP in May."
B "The supplier should have submitted the ITP in May."
C "The supplier should have submit the ITP in May."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modal Perfects

GRAMMAR

How to say what SHOULD have been done, deduce WHY it was not, and disagree without breaking the relationship.

FormFunctionExample
Affirmative
should have + past participle		An obligation that was NOT met. The criticism lands on the action, never on the person."The ITP should have been submitted on June 3rd."
must have + past participleA logical DEDUCTION about the past: from the evidence, you are almost certain. It sounds analytical, not accusatory."There must have been a bottleneck on your side."
might / may have + past participleAn uncertain POSSIBILITY. It opens a door and lets the other side save face."The drawings might have been sent to the wrong contact."
Negative / impossible
could not have + past participle		IMPOSSIBILITY: the evidence rules the hypothesis out."They could not have run the FAT without the cabinet."
QuestionWhat should have happened? / Could it have been lost?"What should have happened after the design review?"
In speech (contraction)The have reduces to /əv/ — that is what you actually hear on a call."It should've been flagged up." · "They must've missed it."
Why it matters"You didn't deliver" attacks a person and the meeting stalls. "This should have been delivered" states a fact about the process — and the supplier can agree without losing face. That is how you walk out of the meeting with a corrective action instead of an enemy.

Watch out (two classic mistakes): (1) after a modal, it is ALWAYS have, never had — "should have submitted", never "should had submitted"; (2) to deduce something about the PAST, the present tense is not enough: "it must be a miscommunication" talks about right now. About the past, the correct form is "there must have been a miscommunication".

Hedging — the language of diplomatic disagreement: "It would appear that..." (instead of "you are wrong"), "One could argue that..." (instead of "that makes no sense"), "To some extent I understand your position, however..." (instead of a flat "no"). These are the three formulas that carry a disagreement without breaking the relationship — and that read well in the minutes your director will see.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The revised baseline schedule us on June 10th."
"There a miscommunication about the hold points."
"The drawings to the wrong contact."
"I would like to the revised baseline schedule."
"I have to that date, it is not realistic."
"Can we agree on a and a new deadline?"

Stage 2: Put the Status Meeting in Order

Order

Put the stages of a status meeting with a late supplier in the correct order.

?Follow up on the overdue item and say what should have happened, and when.
?Agree on a corrective action with a date, and say what happens if the date slips again.
?Thank the supplier for joining and state the agenda: the open items on the punch list.
?Push back diplomatically when the supplier moves the responsibility to you.
?Deduce the cause with a modal perfect, instead of accusing the supplier.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open, carry and close any status meeting. Notice how have contracts in speech: "should've been", "must've been".

I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
The inspection test plan should have been submitted by last Friday.
There must have been a miscommunication about the hold points.
To some extent I understand your position, however I have to push back on that date.
Can we agree on a corrective action and a new deadline?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of a status meeting — including the one where the supplier hands the blame back to you.

You open the status meeting. The revised baseline schedule is three weeks overdue. You say:
A "You are three weeks late. Again."
B "I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule — it should have reached us on June 10th."
C "Where is the schedule? I asked many times."
The supplier blames YOU: "We were waiting for your interface document." It was signed in April. You answer:
A "To some extent I understand your position, however the interface document was signed in April. I have to push back on that."
B "That is a lie and you know it."
C "Ok, maybe it was our fault, let's move on."
The ITP arrived with no hold points, only witness points. The most professional way to raise it is:
A "You removed our hold points on purpose."
B "I'd like to flag up an issue: the ITP has no hold points. That should have been discussed with us before it was submitted."
C "The ITP is wrong. Send it again."
The equipment arrived damaged and nobody knows why. You want to deduce the cause without accusing anyone. You say:
A "Somebody in your factory was careless."
B "There must have been a problem in transport — or it might have been damaged before it left the factory."
C "It must be a problem in transport last week."
The supplier asks for a waiver that saves them three days in the factory but risks three weeks on site. You:
A "Sure, no problem, we can skip that test."
B "One could argue that three days in the factory cost us three weeks during the site acceptance test. I'm afraid I have to push back on that waiver."
C "Waivers are always forbidden here."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. Tone: professional, firm, never confrontational.

Open a status meeting with a supplier who is three weeks late on the revised baseline schedule. Follow up on the overdue item (I'd like to follow up on...), state the obligation that was not met (It should have reached us on...), deduce the cause without accusing anyone (There must have been... / It might have been...), push back diplomatically when the supplier moves the responsibility to you (To some extent I understand your position, however...), and close by agreeing on a corrective action with a new deadline.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1I'd like to follow up on the revised baseline schedule.
2The inspection test plan should have been submitted by last Friday.
3There must have been a miscommunication about the hold points.
4To some extent I understand your position, however I have to push back on that date.
5Can we agree on a corrective action and a new deadline?
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

Navigating Conflict and Escalation -- Diplomatic Assertiveness in High-Stakes Negotiations

The fourth missed deadline: putting the contract on the table without losing the supplier. Key words: contractual obligation, penalty clause, breach of contract, force majeure, liability, indemnity, binding agreement, dispute resolution, mediation, arbitration, to invoke, to put forward, to bite the bullet, a double-edged sword. Structure: inversion for emphasis (Never has... / Not only did... but also... / Hardly had... when... / Under no circumstances will...) + escalation hedging ("It would be remiss of us not to flag that...", "I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to...").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 3 you named the AGENDA of the meeting. Here you name what happens when the meeting fails and the delay turns into a contract matter: what the contract requires, what it costs, and how the dispute ends. Listen to each term and read the example.

Contractual obligation -- something a party is legally required to do under the contract
"This delay puts our contractual obligations at risk."
Penalty clause -- the part of the contract that sets what a party pays for a delay or a failure
"The penalty clause becomes applicable after thirty days."
Breach of contract -- a failure to do what the contract requires, serious enough to have legal consequences
"Three consecutive missed milestones may amount to a breach of contract."
Force majeure -- an extraordinary event beyond anyone's control that excuses a party from performing
"They claimed force majeure after the strike at the port."
Liability -- legal responsibility for a cost, a damage or a loss
"Liability for the delay remains with the supplier."
Indemnity -- a promise written into the contract to compensate the other party for a specific loss
"The indemnity in Clause 9 covers the cost of the re-test."
Binding agreement -- an agreement that both parties are legally obliged to honor
"The minutes of the call became a binding agreement."
Dispute resolution -- the formal process the contract defines for settling a disagreement
"Clause 21 sets out the dispute resolution procedure."
Mediation -- a neutral third party helps both sides reach their own agreement; nothing is imposed
"We proposed mediation before anyone mentioned lawyers."
Arbitration -- a neutral third party hears both sides and imposes a final decision
"Arbitration is faster than court, and the decision is final."
To invoke -- to formally put a clause of the contract into effect
"We are prepared to invoke the penalty clause."
To put forward -- to present a proposal or an argument for the other side to consider
"Let me put forward a recovery plan."
To bite the bullet -- to accept a difficult decision that you have been avoiding
"We had to bite the bullet and re-plan the factory acceptance test."
A double-edged sword -- something with a clear benefit and an equally clear risk
"Escalation is a double-edged sword: it protects the date, and it costs the relationship."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Contractual obligation
Penalty clause
Breach of contract
Force majeure
Liability
Indemnity
Binding agreement
Dispute resolution
Mediation
Arbitration
To invoke
To put forward
To bite the bullet
A double-edged sword

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

The fourth deadline is close, and the file on João Guilherme's desk is no longer a schedule — it is a contract. Never has a supplier on this package missed three consecutive deadlines. The revised baseline schedule was due on 10 June, and it is still outstanding. Not only did the supplier fail to submit it, but they also declined to confirm a new date in writing. Under Clause 14, that is a failure to meet a contractual obligation, and the penalty clause becomes applicable after thirty days.

The supplier blames a strike at the port and has claimed force majeure. Legal disagrees: the strike was announced six weeks in advance, so the liability for the delay remains with the supplier. Hardly had that argument been put forward when the supplier also asked for the indemnity in Clause 9 to be reviewed. Three missed milestones may amount to a breach of contract, but nobody wants to say that word out loud yet.

So João writes the email he has been avoiding. He states the position: "Under no circumstances do we want to invoke the penalty clause — but if the fourth deadline is missed, I am afraid we are left with no alternative." Then he puts forward a recovery plan, proposes mediation before anyone mentions arbitration, and asks for the minutes of the next call to be treated as a binding agreement. Escalation is a double-edged sword: it protects the date and it can cost the relationship. This time he decides to bite the bullet, and the dispute resolution procedure stays in the drawer — for now.

1. "Never has a supplier on this package missed three consecutive deadlines." Why does the verb come BEFORE the subject?
A Because it is a disguised question.
B Because after a negative expression at the start of the sentence (Never), English switches to QUESTION order: auxiliary + subject + verb. That is inversion, and it gives the statement WEIGHT.
C Because the subject is too long and moves to the end.
2. "Not only did they fail to submit the schedule..." Why fail and not failed?
A Because after the auxiliary did the verb goes back to the BASE FORM — the past tense is already carried by did.
B Because the sentence is about the present.
C Because fail is an irregular verb with no past form.
3. Why is "I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke the penalty clause" more effective than "We will fine you"?
A Because it is longer and sounds more formal.
B Because it combines firmness with a way out: it states the contractual consequence without hostility and leaves the door open — the supplier can act without losing face.
C Because it avoids mentioning the penalty.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "Under no circumstances we will accept a fourth revision."
B "Under no circumstances will we accept a fourth revision."
C "Under no circumstances will accept we a fourth revision."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Inversion for Emphasis

GRAMMAR

How to give WEIGHT to a fact without raising your voice — and how to cushion a formal demand.

TriggerStructureExample
Never / Never before+ auxiliary + subject + verb. The word order becomes QUESTION order. It signals that there is no precedent — and that you have been keeping count."Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule."
Not only ... but ... also+ auxiliary + subject + verb in the BASE FORM. With no other auxiliary available, use did. It stacks two failures into one sentence."Not only did they miss the date, but they also sent an incomplete revision."
Hardly / Scarcely ... when
No sooner ... than		+ had + subject + past participle. Two past events almost at the same time — it shows a pattern, not an accident."Hardly had we begun the FAT when the connector failed."
Strong negative
Under no circumstances
At no time / On no account		+ auxiliary + subject. The strongest refusal in professional English. Use it ONCE — and mean it."Under no circumstances will we accept a fourth revision."
QuestionsInversion ALREADY uses question order — which is why it never appears inside a real question. The question itself stays normal: Are you going to invoke the penalty clause?"Are you going to invoke the penalty clause?" (no inversion)
Hedging before the demandThe formula that holds firmness without aggression: a cushion BEFORE the hard fact."It would be remiss of us not to flag that..." · "I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to..."
Why it works"They have never missed three deadlines" is a fact. "Never have they missed three deadlines" is a fact WITH A CASE BEHIND IT. Inversion raises the register and says, in one second, that you have read the contract — and that this email can be forwarded to their director. Use it once or twice per email. Three times sounds theatrical.

Watch out (two classic mistakes): (1) keeping normal word order after the trigger — "Never the supplier has missed..." is wrong; the correct form is "Never has the supplier missed..."; (2) using the past tense after did — "Not only did they failed" is wrong; after the auxiliary comes the base form: "Not only did they fail".

Contract collocations: invoke a penalty clause (never "activate"), claim force majeure, put forward a proposal, pursue a formal complaint, address contractual obligations, reach a resolution, make a compelling case.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule."
" the schedule slip, but no new date has been confirmed in writing."
" we begun the factory acceptance test when a non-conformance was identified."
"Under no circumstances a fourth revision of the schedule."
"I am afraid we are left with no alternative but to the penalty clause."
"Let me a recovery plan before anyone mentions arbitration."

Stage 2: Put the Escalation Call in Order

Order

Put the steps of an escalation call in the right order — from the opening line to the binding agreement.

?Answer the question about the penalty clause: not now, but yes if the fourth deadline is missed.
?Put forward a recovery plan and propose mediation before anyone mentions arbitration.
?Open the call and flag the third missed milestone, with one inversion.
?Close by treating the minutes of the call as a binding agreement.
?State the contractual position: Clause 14, the obligation, and when the penalty clause applies.
?Take the force majeure claim apart with the fact: the strike was announced six weeks in advance.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open, sustain and close an escalation. Notice the stress: it falls on the inversion trigger (NEVER has..., NOT ONLY did...).

It would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone.
Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule.
Not only did the schedule slip, but no new date has been confirmed in writing.
I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke the penalty clause.
We would much rather reach a resolution through mediation than pursue a formal complaint.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best reply for each real moment of a conflict negotiation — including the one where the supplier asks you, point blank, whether you will invoke the penalty.

You open the escalation call. It is the third missed milestone on the package. You say:
A "This is the third time. Do you people ever deliver anything?"
B "It would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone. Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule."
C "Sorry to bother you again, but could we maybe have an update, if it is not too much trouble?"
The supplier claims force majeure. The strike at the port was announced six weeks in advance. You answer:
A "One could argue that a strike announced six weeks in advance does not meet the definition of force majeure in Clause 14. On that basis, liability for the delay remains with you."
B "That excuse is ridiculous and you know it."
C "Ok, if it was a strike, there is nothing we can do."
She asks you directly: "Are you going to invoke the penalty clause?" The best answer is:
A "Yes. The letter goes out today."
B "No, no, of course not. Let's not talk about that."
C "Under no circumstances do I want to start there. But if the fourth deadline is missed, I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke it."
They missed the date AND sent an incomplete revision. You want both facts in one sentence, with weight:
A "Not only did they miss the date, but they also sent an incomplete revision."
B "Not only they missed the date, but they also sent an incomplete revision."
C "They missed the date. Also the revision was incomplete. Also nobody called."
You want to propose a way out before anyone mentions lawyers. You say:
A "If this is not fixed by Friday, we will see you in arbitration."
B "Let me put forward a recovery plan. We would much rather reach a resolution through mediation than pursue a formal complaint."
C "We will decide internally and let you know what we have decided."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the prompt below. Speak for two minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. Tone: firm on the contract, open on the relationship.

Open an escalation call with a supplier who has missed a third milestone. Flag it with one inversion (Never has... / Not only did...), state the contractual position (Under Clause 14... the penalty clause becomes applicable after thirty days), answer the force majeure claim with the fact (the strike was announced six weeks in advance), say what happens if the fourth deadline is missed (I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to...), and close by putting forward a recovery plan and proposing mediation before anyone mentions arbitration.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1It would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone.
2Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule.
3Not only did the schedule slip, but no new date has been confirmed in writing.
4I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke the penalty clause.
5We would much rather reach a resolution through mediation than pursue a formal complaint.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

Performing Under Pressure -- The International Job Interview

The international interview: how to turn seven years of experience into EVIDENCE, control what the panel hears first, and answer the uncomfortable question without apologizing. Key words: track record, cross-functional, high-stakes, resilience, stakeholder management, value proposition, technical lead, subject matter expert, performance-driven, results-oriented, to set out, to account for, to burn the midnight oil, to throw in the towel. Structure: cleft sentences ("What I bring to this role is...", "It was in San Francisco that...", "The reason I... is that...") + the four interview answer patterns.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Lessons 2 to 4 gave a name to the work: the actions, the inspection, the contract. This one gives a name to YOU — the words an interview panel is waiting to hear. You have already DONE all of this at Motiva; what is missing is the English label. Listen to each term and read the example.

Track record -- a documented history of results you can point to, with dates and numbers
"I can demonstrate a track record of delivering signaling packages on time."
Cross-functional -- involving people from different departments and disciplines at the same time
"I lead cross-functional teams: signaling, civil works and the client."
High-stakes -- where the consequences of getting it wrong are serious and public
"Signaling is a high-stakes discipline: the failure mode is a collision."
Resilience -- the ability to keep performing when the pressure does not stop
"What that year taught me was resilience, not patience."
Stakeholder management -- keeping everyone with an interest in the project informed, aligned and calm
"Stakeholder management is half the job: the client, the supplier and the regulator."
Value proposition -- the one concrete reason to choose you instead of the other candidate
"My value proposition is simple: I read the standard and I run the site."
Technical lead -- the engineer who owns the technical decisions on a project, and answers for them
"I was the technical lead on the Line 5 signaling package."
Subject matter expert -- the person the team goes to when the answer is not in the manual
"On interlocking logic, I am the subject matter expert for my team."
Performance-driven -- working against a number: a metric, a KPI, a measurable target
"It is a performance-driven environment: every milestone has an indicator."
Results-oriented -- asking to be judged by what you delivered, not by how busy you looked
"I am results-oriented: judge me by the commissioning date, not by my hours."
To set out -- to explain your background clearly and in order, so the listener can follow it
"Let me set out my experience in three steps."
To account for -- to explain a decision or a period that somebody is entitled to ask about
"I can account for the two years when I stopped using English."
To burn the midnight oil -- to work late into the night to finish something that matters (informal)
"We burned the midnight oil for a week before the factory acceptance test."
To throw in the towel -- to give up when it gets hard -- the thing you say you did NOT do (informal)
"That interview went badly, but I did not throw in the towel."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Track record
Cross-functional
High-stakes
Resilience
Stakeholder management
Value proposition
Technical lead
Subject matter expert
Performance-driven
Results-oriented
To set out
To account for
To burn the midnight oil
To throw in the towel

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

It is four o'clock on a Tuesday and João Guilherme is sitting in front of a panel of two. Sofie Claes, Head of Signaling Engineering, has twenty minutes and a shortlist of five engineers, all of them with seven years of experience. Experience is time, and everybody on that list has it. What wins the room is a track record: a project, a decision, an outcome.

He opens carefully. "Let me set out my background in three steps. What I do at Motiva is own the signaling design for metro line extensions, from the specification to commissioning. What I bring to this role is seven years of managing German, French and Chinese suppliers under contractual pressure." Sofie is not impressed yet. She asks the question that decides everything: what makes him the right person, and not the other four? "What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site. Most engineers do one or the other. It was in San Francisco, in 2023, that I did both on the same project." That is a value proposition, and it takes one sentence.

Then comes the uncomfortable question, the one he did not prepare in March: there was a period when he was not using English. He does not apologize. He accounts for it. "What I learned from that experience was that fluency is a habit, not a memory. It was an interview, in March, that made me rebuild the habit." He did not throw in the towel, and the word for that, in an interview, is resilience. Stakeholder management, a cross-functional team, a high-stakes discipline where the failure mode is a collision: the engineering was always there. Tonight, so was the English.

1. "What I bring to this role is seven years in signaling." Why is the verb is, and not are, if "seven years" is plural?
A Because What... works like "the thing that" — a SINGULAR subject. The verb agrees with it, never with what follows.
B Because English drops subject-verb agreement after what.
C Because "seven years" is treated like an amount of money, so it turns singular.
2. "It was in San Francisco that I ran my first commissioning cycle." What does this structure DO to the sentence?
A It adds a new piece of information about San Francisco.
B It puts ONE fact under the spotlight — the place — and tells the panel where to look. The information is the same; the weight is not.
C It makes the sentence more formal, but the effect is purely decorative.
3. Why does "What I bring to this role is seven years..." work better in an interview than "I have seven years of experience"?
A Because it is longer, and long sentences sound more professional.
B Because it announces that something important is coming, saves the decisive information for the END — and buys you half a second to think.
C Because it avoids the verb have, which is far too informal.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "It was in San Francisco where I ran my first commissioning cycle."
B "What I bring to this role, it is deep expertise in signaling."
C "It was in San Francisco that I ran my first commissioning cycle."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Cleft Sentences

GRAMMAR

How to split a sentence in two so that you control what the panel hears first — and buy yourself half a second to think.

FormFunctionExample
wh-cleft
What + subject + verb + is + the point		You ANNOUNCE that something important is coming, and only then deliver it. The panel leans forward. The listener knows a headline is on its way."What I bring to this role is seven years in signaling."
it-cleft
It was + the fact + that + the rest		You put ONE fact under the spotlight: a place, a date, a person, a decision. The linking word is always that — never where, never when."It was in San Francisco that I ran my first commissioning cycle."
The reason + clause + is thatYou take control of the story behind a decision before anybody reads it the wrong way."The reason I moved into signaling is that safety is non-negotiable."
AgreementAfter What..., the verb is ALWAYS is / was, even in front of a plural — because What means "the thing that", a singular subject."What I bring is seven years." (never are)
Question / negativeIt behaves the same way: What he wants is a date. / What I do not do is promise dates I cannot defend."What I do not do is promise a date I cannot defend."
In speechThe pause falls right BEFORE the is. That pause is thinking time — and it belongs to you."What I learned from that experience … was that fluency is a habit."
Why it mattersUnder pressure, a nervous candidate dumps everything he knows into one long sentence — and the panel stops listening. A cleft sentence does the opposite: it tells the panel where to look. Same information, completely different candidate.

Watch out (two classic mistakes): (1) the repeated pronoun — the subject pronoun does NOT come back after the cleft: "What I bring to this role it is..." is wrong; the correct form is "What I bring to this role is..."; (2) the where — the English it-cleft demands that, even when you are pointing at a place: "It was in San Francisco that I ran my first commissioning cycle."

The four interview patterns: "One example that comes to mind is..." (the behavioral question), "What I learned from that experience was..." (the closing line of any story), "The way I approach this kind of challenge is..." (the method question) and "I would say my greatest strength in this area is..." (the direct question). Four skeletons: all you change is the filling.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

" seven years in railway signaling."
" I ran my first full commissioning cycle."
" that I read the standard and I run the site."
"I can the two years when I stopped using English."
"Let me my background in three steps."
"I can demonstrate a of delivering signaling packages on time."

Stage 2: Put the Interview in Order

Order

Put the stages of an international interview answer in the right order.

?State your value proposition in one cleft sentence: what sets you apart from the other candidates.
?Close by asking the panel a question only an engineer would ask.
?Set out your background in three steps, starting from the value and not from the biography.
?Prove it with a track record: one project, the decision you took, and the outcome.
?Account for the uncomfortable question with evidence, not with an apology.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open, carry and close any interview. Notice the pause that falls right BEFORE the is — it belongs to you, and it is thinking time.

Let me set out my background in three steps.
What I bring to this role is seven years of managing international suppliers.
What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site.
It was in San Francisco, in 2023, that I ran my first full commissioning cycle.
I can account for it, and what I learned was that fluency is a habit, not a memory.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the strongest answer for each real moment of an international interview — including the uncomfortable question about your English.

The panel opens with "Tell me about yourself." You have ninety seconds. You start with:
A "I was born in São Paulo and I studied electrical engineering..."
B "Let me set out my background in three steps. What I do at Motiva is own the signaling design for metro line extensions."
C "Well, it is difficult to summarize seven years, but I will try."
"Everyone on this shortlist has seven years. What makes you the right person?" You answer:
A "What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site. Most engineers do one or the other."
B "I am very dedicated and I always give my best."
C "I think my experience speaks for itself."
She asks for a conflict with an international supplier. The strongest opening is:
A "Suppliers are always late, it happens on every project."
B "One example that comes to mind is a supplier who missed three consecutive deadlines. What I did was put forward a recovery plan before anyone mentioned the penalty clause."
C "I would have to think about that one for a moment."
"You were not using English intensively for a while. How do I know that will not be a problem?" You answer:
A "I am sorry about my English, I know it is not perfect."
B "My English is fine, it was only a bad day in that interview."
C "I can account for it. What I learned from that period was that fluency is a habit, not a memory — and I have been in weekly training since March."
The technical director asks how you handle competing priorities. He wants ONE sentence, not three minutes. You say:
A "The way I approach this kind of challenge is to ask which item is on the critical path for safety approval."
B "It depends. Let me explain the relay logic first, and then the interlocking table, and then..."
C "I work hard and I burn the midnight oil until everything is done."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. Tone: professional, confident, straight to the value.

"Tell me about yourself." You have ninety seconds with an international panel. Set out your background in three steps (Let me set out...), say what you bring to the role (What I bring to this role is...), prove it with one project (It was in San Francisco, in 2023, that...), state what makes you different from the other candidates (What sets me apart is...), and finish by accounting for the period when your English went quiet (I can account for it. What I learned from that experience was...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Let me set out my background in three steps.
2What I bring to this role is seven years of managing international suppliers.
3What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site.
4One example that comes to mind is a supplier who missed three consecutive deadlines.
5I can account for it, and what I learned was that fluency is a habit, not a memory.
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

Holding Your Ground -- The Supplier Approval Meeting

The safety relay failed the factory acceptance test and the supplier calls it a "minor deviation": how to state the failure without accusing anybody, how to demand the corrective action without doing the work yourself, and how to keep the hold point closed. Key words: deviation, submittal, approval workflow, inspection release, out of tolerance, calibration certificate, traceability, root cause, rework, sign-off, to downplay, to raise a concern, to hold your ground, to pass the buck. Structure: the advanced passive (was found to be... / had not been issued / must be implemented) + the causative have something done ("We need to have the report resubmitted by Friday").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 5 you put a name to yourself in front of an interview panel. Here you put a name to the DEFECT — and to whoever pays for it. These are the words of the approval meeting: what was found, what had not been issued yet, and what the supplier will try to call "minor". Listen to every term and read the example.

Deviation -- a departure from what the approved specification requires
"The deviation was accepted in writing, so no rework is required."
Submittal -- a document the supplier formally sends to the client for review and approval
"The submittal was returned with comments on Tuesday."
Approval workflow -- the sequence of reviews a document must pass before it is accepted
"The drawing is stuck in the approval workflow, not on my desk."
Inspection release -- the formal authorization to ship, or to move to the next stage, after an inspection
"No inspection release has been issued, so nothing leaves the factory."
Out of tolerance -- outside the range of values the specification allows
"The relay was found to be out of tolerance during the functional test."
Calibration certificate -- the document that proves an instrument was verified against a reference
"The calibration certificate had not been issued at the time of the test."
Traceability -- being able to track a part back to its material, its test and its records
"In signaling, a component without traceability does not exist."
Root cause -- the underlying reason a failure happened, not the symptom you can see
"The root cause has not been identified yet, so the corrective action is premature."
Rework -- work that has to be done a second time to correct a defect
"The rework will be carried out at the supplier's own cost."
Sign-off -- the formal written approval that closes a stage and lets the project move on
"I cannot give you sign-off until the calibration certificate is on file."
To downplay -- to make a problem sound smaller than it really is
"He downplayed the failure: 'It is a minor deviation, nothing more.'"
To raise a concern -- to put a risk formally on the record, before it becomes a problem
"I would like to raise a concern about the traceability of that batch."
To hold your ground -- to keep your position when the other side pushes back
"He downplayed it twice, and twice I held my ground."
To pass the buck -- to shift responsibility to somebody else instead of owning it (informal)
"Blaming the subcontractor is passing the buck: our contract is with you."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match every term with the right definition.

Deviation
Submittal
Approval workflow
Inspection release
Out of tolerance
Calibration certificate
Traceability
Root cause
Rework
Sign-off
To downplay
To raise a concern
To hold your ground
To pass the buck

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text, then answer the questions below.

The factory acceptance test was carried out on a Tuesday morning, with João Guilherme watching on a video link from São Paulo. The safety relay was found to be out of tolerance: eleven milliseconds, against a specified range of five to seven. The test was witnessed, a hold point was triggered, and no inspection release was issued. Then the report arrived from the supplier, and every sentence in it had been written to make the failure disappear.

Two things had not been done at the time of the test. The calibration certificate for the reference instrument had not been issued, and the root cause had not been identified. The report calls the failure a minor deviation and asks for a waiver, which is the polite way of asking João to pay for somebody else's rework. On the call, the quality manager downplays it — "the unit works" — and then passes the buck: the calibration, she says, was performed by a subcontractor. João holds his ground. A deviation is approved before the work; this was found after it. The contract is with her company, not with the subcontractor.

So he states what has to happen, and he states it in the passive, because the obligation belongs to the procedure and not to him. "A corrective action must be implemented before the retest. We need to have the report resubmitted through the approval workflow by Friday, and we will have the relay retested with a valid certificate in place. I would also like to raise a concern about the traceability of that batch. Sign-off cannot be granted while the hold point remains open." Nobody was accused. Nothing was denied. And the relay is being retested on Thursday.

1. "The relay was found to be out of tolerance." Why does the engineer choose the passive here, instead of "Your technician calibrated it badly"?
A Because the passive is more polite, even though it is weaker.
B Because the passive takes the PERSON out of the sentence and leaves the FACT. An accusation can be disputed and defended; a finding can only be CLOSED — and that is why every inspection report is written this way.
C Because the active voice is not allowed in technical English.
2. "The calibration certificate had not been issued at the time of the test." What does the PERFECT passive do here that the simple passive would not do?
A It dates the absence: at the MOMENT of the test, that document did not exist yet — so it does not cover the test. Issuing it afterwards solves nothing.
B It only sounds more formal; the meaning is the same as "was not issued".
C It means the certificate will never be issued.
3. "We need to have the report resubmitted by Friday." WHO resubmits the report?
A I do, because have means "to have to do something".
B Nobody — the sentence only describes an intention.
C THE SUPPLIER. The causative have something done says the action is DEMANDED by me and CARRIED OUT by somebody else. I do not do the work; I require it.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "We need to have resubmitted the report by Friday."
B "We need to have the report resubmitted by Friday."
C "We need to have the report resubmit by Friday."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Advanced Passive & Causative

GRAMMAR

How to state a failure without accusing a person — and how to demand the fix without doing the work yourself.

FormFunctionExample
Simple passive
be + participle		The object becomes the subject and the agent DROPS OUT — because it is obvious, because it is unknown, or because naming it would start a fight."The submittal was returned with comments."
Passive of a finding
be + found / reported / believed + to be		The register of every inspection report. You state the FACT, not the person. Note: is believed to have been caused is NOT the same as has been identified — the supplier reaches for the first one precisely in order not to commit to the second."The relay was found to be out of tolerance."
Perfect passive
had been + participle		It dates the ABSENCE: at the time of the test, that document did not exist yet. This is the sentence that stops the supplier from making the problem go away by issuing the certificate afterwards."The certificate had not been issued at the time of the test."
Modal passive
modal + be + participle		The obligation belongs to the PROCEDURE, not to you — and that is why nobody takes it personally. After the modal comes be, always."A corrective action must be implemented." · "Sign-off cannot be granted."
Causative
have + OBJECT + participle		You DEMAND the action; somebody else CARRIES IT OUT. The order is fixed: the thing comes before the participle. get something done is the same, only less formal."We need to have the report resubmitted." · "I will get the relay retested."
Causative with a person
have somebody + BASE FORM		For when you DO need to name who does the work. Then the verb goes back to the base form — no to, no participle."I will have your team resubmit the report."
Why it matters"You calibrated it badly" is an accusation — and everything that follows it will be a defense. "The relay was found to be out of tolerance" is the SAME fact with the person removed: nobody is attacked and nothing can be denied. Then the causative hands the work back to whoever owns it: "we need to have it retested" — not by us, by THEM, and by Friday.

Watch out (the two classic traps): (1) dropping the be or the participle after the modal — "The report must be resubmit" and "A corrective action must implemented" are both wrong; the right forms are "must be resubmitted" and "must be implemented"; (2) the word order of the causative — the temptation is to say "We need to have resubmitted the report", which in English is a different tense and a different meaning altogether. In the causative the OBJECT comes FIRST: "have the report resubmitted".

Collocations of the approval meeting: carry out a test, issue a certificate (NEVER "emit" — the classic false friend), trigger a hold point, identify the root cause, raise a concern, grant sign-off, resubmit a report through the approval workflow.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete every sentence with the right structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The relay out of tolerance during the functional test."
"The calibration certificate at the time of the test."
"A corrective action before the retest."
"We need to by Friday."
"Sign-off while the hold point remains open."
"The has not been identified yet, so the corrective action is premature."

Stage 2: Put the Approval Meeting in Order

Order

Put the stages of a hold-point meeting in the right order — from the finding to the refusal of sign-off.

?Put the responsibility back where the contract is, when the supplier blames the subcontractor.
?Demand the work with the causative, and give the date: have the report resubmitted by Friday.
?Hold the gate: sign-off cannot be granted while the hold point remains open.
?Refuse the word: a deviation is approved before the work, so a failure found after it is a non-conformance.
?State the finding in the passive: the relay was found to be out of tolerance during the functional test.
?Date what is missing: the calibration certificate had not been issued at the time of the test.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to every sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open, hold and close an approval meeting. Watch the stress: it falls on the participle (was FOUND to be out of TOLerance) — and "certificate" ends in -kit, unstressed, never -keit.

The relay was found to be out of tolerance during the functional test.
The calibration certificate had not been issued at the time of the test.
A corrective action must be implemented before the retest.
We need to have the report resubmitted by Friday.
Sign-off cannot be granted while the hold point remains open.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for every real moment of a factory acceptance test meeting — including the moment when the supplier downplays the failure or shifts the blame onto the subcontractor.

The supplier's quality manager opens with "It is a minor deviation, the unit works." You answer:
A "A deviation is approved before the work. This was found after it, on a safety relay, so I am afraid that is a non-conformance."
B "You are wrong and you know it. That relay failed."
C "All right, if the unit works, I suppose we can look at it as a deviation."
She says the certificate was issued the Monday AFTER the test. The strongest reply is:
A "That is good news, we can close the point then."
B "The calibration certificate had not been issued at the time of the test, so it does not cover the test. The unit will have to be retested with a valid certificate in place."
C "Please send it to me and I will see what I can do."
"The calibration was performed by our subcontractor, not by us." You answer:
A "Then I will take it up with your subcontractor directly."
B "That is not my problem, and it is not an excuse."
C "To some extent, I understand your position. However, our contract is with you, so the root cause still has to be identified on your side."
She asks what you need before the unit can be released. You want the work done by THEM, with a date:
A "We need to have the report resubmitted through the approval workflow by Friday, with the root cause and a corrective action plan."
B "We need to have resubmitted the report by Friday."
C "I will resubmit the report myself once you send me the data."
She asks for a provisional sign-off so the shipment is not delayed. You say no without closing the door:
A "I cannot sign anything, please stop asking me."
B "I am afraid sign-off cannot be granted while the hold point remains open. As soon as the retest passes and the certificate is on file, the inspection release can be issued the same day."
C "All right, provisionally, but do not tell anyone."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two minutes, with no script and with no stopping to correct yourself. Tone: technical, impersonal in the finding, firm in the demand.

You are opening a hold-point meeting with the supplier's quality manager. In ninety seconds: state what was found during the functional test (The relay was found to be...), state what had not been done at the time of the test (The calibration certificate had not been...), refuse the word "deviation" and give the criterion, demand the root cause and the corrective action with a date (We need to have the report resubmitted...), and close by saying why sign-off cannot be granted yet.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1The relay was found to be out of tolerance during the functional test.
2The calibration certificate had not been issued at the time of the test.
3It would appear that the deviation was caused by the calibration drift.
4We need to have the report resubmitted by Friday.
5Sign-off cannot be granted while the hold point remains open.
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

Never Back Down -- The Quarterly Project Review

Six weeks of slippage on the installation, and a supplier who remembers the promise differently: how to open the meeting with the NUMBER, how to turn memory into a RECORD (date + reporting verb + backshift), and how to concede what is true without handing over the float of the project. Key words: schedule slippage, variance, critical path, float, recovery schedule, contingency plan, risk mitigation, post-mortem review, steering committee, buy-in, to make headway, to reach a consensus, to take a stance, to bring about. Structure: reported speech and reporting verbs (On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship... / your company undertook to submit...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 6 you named the defect. Here you name the DELAY — and who pays for it. These are the words that separate a vague complaint from a schedule you can defend in a room: the variance, the critical path, the float that is not there. Listen to each term and read the example.

Schedule slippage -- time lost against the plan, a few days at a time, until the date is gone
"The schedule slippage on the installation package is now six weeks."
Variance -- the measured difference between the plan and reality -- the number in the report
"The schedule variance is minus thirty working days against the plan."
Critical path -- the chain of activities that decides the end date: delay any of them and the project moves
"Signal installation is on the critical path, so this delay is not absorbable."
Float -- the spare time an activity has before it starts delaying the whole project
"That activity had ten days of float. The supplier has used all of it."
Recovery schedule -- the revised plan that shows exactly how the lost time will be won back
"A recovery schedule is required within ten days, not another apology."
Contingency plan -- what you do if the recovery fails -- the plan you hope not to use
"Our contingency plan is to commission the line in two stages instead of one."
Risk mitigation -- action taken now to make a risk smaller, before it becomes a delay
"Dual sourcing the cable was risk mitigation, and it is why we are only six weeks late."
Post-mortem review -- a session held after the fact, to find out why it happened and never repeat it
"We will hold a post-mortem review once the line is commissioned, not now."
Steering committee -- the senior group that decides what the project team is not allowed to decide alone
"If we cannot agree a date today, this goes to the steering committee in April."
Buy-in -- real agreement from the people who could block you -- not just their signature
"I have the approval. What I do not have yet is buy-in from the operations team."
To make headway -- to make real progress against something that is pushing back
"We are finally making headway on the interface drawings."
To reach a consensus -- to arrive at a position that everybody in the room can live with
"We reached a consensus on the sequence, not on the date."
To take a stance -- to state a position publicly, and then stand behind it when it is challenged
"Motiva has taken a stance on the delay: no payment against an unapproved schedule."
To bring about -- to cause something to happen, usually after effort and resistance
"It took two escalations to bring about a resolution."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Schedule slippage
Variance
Critical path
Float
Recovery schedule
Contingency plan
Risk mitigation
Post-mortem review
Steering committee
Buy-in
To make headway
To reach a consensus
To take a stance
To bring about

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

The quarterly review opens at ten, and Elena Vargas opens it with the good news: her team is working weekends and they are finally making headway on site. João Guilherme lets her finish, and then he puts the number on the table. The schedule variance on the signaling installation is minus thirty working days. The activity sits on the critical path, so the float is zero and nothing absorbs it. Six weeks of schedule slippage, and the passenger waits six weeks longer.

Elena has been preparing her version for a month. The interface drawings arrived late from Motiva, she says, and her team could not start. She is partly right, and João says so: to some extent, both parties share responsibility, and the drawings were issued eight days late. Then she goes further. I never promised you the fifteenth, she says. I said we would do our best. And this is the moment the meeting is won or lost — not by raising the voice, but by reading the record. On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth, and the minutes were circulated to you that same afternoon.
