In the last review your team told us that the installation was on track. Your planner claimed that the cable had already been dispatched; the carrier's records show it left the warehouse eleven days later. And on 12 June your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days. Twenty-two days have passed, and nothing has been received.

Elena stops. That is fair, she acknowledges. The schedule was not sent, and I should have called you. Nobody was accused, nothing was denied, and the argument about memory is over — because a dated record cannot be argued with, only explained. So she asks what he needs. He asks for three things: a recovery schedule by Friday, a contingency plan if the retest fails, and a weekly call, so that the next slip reaches him in three days and not in three weeks. She asks for one thing back: two weeks of float from the testing activity. He refuses. The float belongs to the project. And if they can reach a consensus today, none of this has to go to the steering committee in April.

1. "On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship." Why does the verb step back from will to would?
A Because would is more polite than will.
B Because the backshift marks the sentence as a QUOTE of what SHE said, not a promise JOAO is making now. Without it ("you assured us the relay will ship"), the sentence stops being her record and turns into his own commitment.
C Because will is not allowed in technical English.
2. Elena says "I never promised you the fifteenth". What ENDS the argument?
A Raising your voice and repeating that she promised.
B The RECORD: the date (3 June), the reporting verb (assured us) and the minutes circulated that same afternoon. A dated record cannot be argued with — it can only be explained.
C Escalating to the steering committee straight away.
3. "Your planner claimed that the cable had already been dispatched." What does CLAIMED communicate that SAID would not?
A That Joao DOUBTS the statement — claim marks what was said as something that did not hold up. Said would be neutral; claimed already opens the check.
B That the planner shouted.
C That the statement was official and has been proven.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "Your company assured us to submit a recovery schedule within ten days."
B "Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days."
C "Your company said us that it would submit a recovery schedule within ten days."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech & Reporting Verbs

GRAMMAR

How to turn what the other side said into a record — and why the reporting verb you pick decides how tightly it holds them.

They saidFunctionYou report
will → wouldThe promise steps back one tense — and becomes a RECORD of what they promised, not a promise of your own."You assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth."
is / are → was / wereTheir present becomes your past: it was true for them, at that moment."Your team told us that the installation was on track."
has been → had beenThe perfect becomes the past perfect. This is the line that DATES a claim — and a dated claim is one you can check."Your planner claimed that the cable had been dispatched."
can → could · must → had toModals step back too. Must is the exception: it becomes had to."They said they could recover two weeks, and that we had to wait."
say x tellTELL needs the person (told us / told me). SAY does NOT take the person directly: it is said that or said TO me. "He said me" does not exist."He told us that..." · "He said that..."
The verb picks the WEIGHTsaid (neutral) · claimed (you doubt it) · assured us (they committed, with confidence) · acknowledged / conceded (they admitted it against their own interest) · denied (they denied it) · undertook / committed to (a formal obligation)."They assured us" holds them. "They said" does not.
What it is for"You are always late" is an accusation — and an accusation gets denied, defended and argued over for three weeks. "On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth" is the SAME fact turned into a RECORD: it has a date, a reporting verb, a backshift. A record cannot be argued with — it can only be explained. And whoever explains is already negotiating.

Watch out (three mistakes Brazilian speakers make): (1) "He said me that..." — SAY never takes the person: it is told me (or said to me); (2) NOT stepping the verb back — "he said the relay will ship" stops quoting the other side and turns into a promise of YOUR OWN; (3) "They assured us TO send the schedule" — assure takes that + clause; the verbs that take the infinitive are undertake / commit / promise: "they undertook to send the schedule".

Collocations of the schedule review: compress the schedule (never "reduce"), secure buy-in, reach a consensus (never "arrive at"), mitigate a risk, make headway (NEVER "have progress"), absorb a delay, take a stance, bring about a resolution.

Hedging (to concede without giving ground): "To some extent, both parties share responsibility for..." · "One could argue that the delay was partly caused by..." · "That is fair, and I will record it. It does not change what is on the critical path."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"On 3 June you the relay would ship on the fifteenth."
"In the last review your team the installation was on track."
"Your planner claimed that the cable ."
"Your company a recovery schedule within ten days."
"The activity is on the , so there is no float to absorb the delay."
"If we can today, this does not have to go to the steering committee."

Stage 2: Put the Review Meeting in Order

Order

Put the steps of a quarterly schedule review in the correct order — from the number to the consequence.

?Say why nothing absorbs it: the activity is on the critical path and the float is zero.
?Close with the demand and the consequence: a recovery schedule by Friday, or the steering committee in April.
?Put the promise on the record with the date: on 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship.
?Open with the number: the schedule variance is minus thirty working days.
?Name the obligation that was never met: your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule.
?Concede what is true: to some extent, both parties share responsibility for the late drawings.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open, hold and close a schedule review. Watch the stress: "VA-ri-ance" (on the first syllable, not "va-ri-AN-ce") and "com-MIT-tee" with a short -ee. And say SKED-jool, not SHED-jool.

The schedule variance is minus thirty working days, and the activity is on the critical path.
On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth.
Your planner claimed that the cable had already been dispatched.
Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days.
To some extent, both parties share responsibility. The float, however, belongs to the project.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the strongest answer for each real moment of the quarterly review — including the moment the supplier denies the promise, and the moment she asks for the float of your project.

Elena opens the meeting with the good news ("the team is working weekends"). You reply:
A "Thank you, Elena. Let me start with the number, and then I would like to hear you. The schedule variance on the installation is minus thirty working days, and the activity is on the critical path."
B "Weekends are the minimum I expect at this point."
C "That is good to hear. So, how are things going in general?"
"I never promised you the fifteenth. I said we would do our best." The strongest reply is:
A "You did promise it, and everybody in that call heard you."
B "That is why I am reading from the minutes. On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth, and the minutes were circulated to you the same afternoon."
C "All right, perhaps I misunderstood you. Let us move on."
"The interface drawings arrived late from your side." She is PARTLY right. You reply:
A "That has nothing to do with the delay we are discussing."
B "You are right, and I accept that the delay is shared."
C "To some extent, both parties share responsibility, and the drawings were issued eight days late. The slippage we are discussing, however, is thirty days."
"Give us two weeks of float from the testing activity and we recover everything." You refuse:
A "I am afraid the float belongs to the project, not to the package that is late. If I hand it over now, I have nothing left when something unplanned happens."
B "Fine, take the two weeks, but this is the last time."
C "I do not have the authority to discuss float with you."
She asks you not to escalate the meeting. You say yes ON YOUR OWN TERMS:
A "I cannot promise anything, it is out of my hands."
B "If we can reach a consensus today — a recovery schedule by Friday and a contingency plan — this does not have to go to the steering committee in April."
C "Do not worry, I will keep this between us whatever happens."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and no stopping to correct yourself. Tone: precise on the number, even-handed in the concession, firm on the record.

You are chairing the quarterly review with a supplier who is six weeks behind. In ninety seconds: open with the variance and say why the float does not absorb it, concede the part that is genuinely yours (the drawings were eight days late), put two commitments on the record with their dates (On 3 June you assured us that... / On 12 June your company undertook to...), refuse to hand over the float from the testing activity, and close by saying what you need by Friday and what happens if it does not arrive.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1The schedule variance is minus thirty working days, and the activity is on the critical path.
2On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth.
3Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days.
4To some extent, both parties share responsibility. The float, however, belongs to the project.
5If we can reach a consensus today, this does not have to go to the steering committee.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

Say Exactly What You Mean -- Writing the RFI

Six interlocking units failed the EMC test — and all forty were built to the same grounding configuration. How to write the RFI that locks the scope onto the six, puts the fact about the forty on record, and cannot be read in two ways. Key words: request for information (RFI), request for proposal (RFP), interoperability, electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), grounding, interlocking, fail-safe, redundancy, acceptance criteria, workaround, ambiguity, to spell out, to devise, to pin down. Structure: relative clauses — defining vs. non-defining (the comma that rewrites the contract), reduced relatives, and preposition + which.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In lesson 7 you turned memory into a record. Here you write the document — and a technical document is not read, it is interpreted. These are the words of the formal clarification: what you ask, what you measure, and what you accept only as a temporary arrangement. Listen to each term and read the example.

Request for information (RFI) -- a formal written question sent to a supplier when a document does not tell you enough
"The RFI that we submitted on Monday has not been answered."
Request for proposal (RFP) -- a formal invitation asking suppliers to propose a solution, with a price, for work not yet awarded
"The rework is inside the contract, so this is an RFI, not an RFP."
Interoperability -- the ability of two different systems to work together and understand each other
"Each system passed alone. What has not been demonstrated is interoperability."
Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) -- whether equipment can operate without disturbing other equipment through electrical noise
"Six units failed the electromagnetic compatibility test on 14 July."
Grounding -- the connection that gives electrical noise a safe path away from the equipment
"It was the grounding configuration, common to all forty units, that caused the interference."
Interlocking -- the signaling logic that makes two conflicting movements impossible at the same time
"The interlocking units that failed are on the northbound platform."
Fail-safe -- designed so that when it fails, it fails into the state that is safe
"A signal that loses power goes to red. That is what fail-safe means."
Redundancy -- a second, independent path that keeps the system working when the first one fails
"The redundancy that protects the northbound line has not been demonstrated."
Acceptance criteria -- the written conditions a deliverable must meet before anybody is allowed to accept it
"The acceptance criteria that apply to this test are in clause 7.4, not in the email."
Workaround -- a temporary way of operating that lets you carry on while the real fix is still missing
"A workaround is acceptable for the trial run. It is not acceptable for the handover."
Ambiguity -- when a sentence is clear, but can honestly be understood in two different ways
"The ambiguity in clause 7.4 is what the supplier is relying on."
To spell out -- to state something in complete detail, leaving nothing for the reader to assume
"The RFI has to spell out which units, against which criterion, and by when."
To devise -- to think out and design a solution that did not exist before
"The workaround that your team devised is not written down anywhere."
To pin down -- to identify something exactly, when it has been vague or moving until now
"Until we pin down the source of the interference, any fix is a guess."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Request for information (RFI)
Request for proposal (RFP)
Interoperability
Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC)
Grounding
Interlocking
Fail-safe
Redundancy
Acceptance criteria
Workaround
Ambiguity
To spell out
To devise
To pin down

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

Forty interlocking units were delivered for Line 5, and on 14 July six of them failed the electromagnetic compatibility test. João Guilherme has to write the request for information that goes to the German supplier, and he has one afternoon to decide what the document actually asks for. The six that failed are the six installed closest to the traction return cable. But all forty were built to the same grounding configuration — and that is the sentence that will cost somebody money.

Read the two versions carefully, because they contain the same words. "The interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked." No commas: the clause RESTRICTS. It says WHICH units — those six, and nobody else. Now the second: "The interlocking units, which failed the EMC test, must be reworked." Two commas, and the clause no longer restricts anything: it simply ADDS a fact about all of them. It says that all forty failed. And all forty did not fail, so João has just written a claim he cannot prove, and the first person to notice will be their lawyer. One comma. Thirty-four units.

So he writes it the way it has to be written. The six units that failed are to be reworked at the supplier's cost. The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified — a fact about the whole batch, stated as a fact, without pretending the whole batch failed. Then he compresses, the way every standard on his shelf compresses: the report issued on 14 July does not cover the configuration, and the units radiating above the limit are the six on the northbound platform. And he closes with the two questions that no ambiguity survives: please confirm the standard to which the design was certified, and spell out the conditions under which the EMC test was performed — on the bench, or as installed? The supplier's answer arrives nine days later. Their team has devised a workaround, ferrite filters, and they are careful too: the filters will meet the acceptance criteria applicable to the trial run. Not to the handover. In a technical document, nobody writes a relative clause by accident.

1. "The interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked." How many units does the supplier have to rework?
A All forty — the clause simply describes the units in general.
B SIX — with no commas, the clause DEFINES: it says WHICH units. Take the clause out and you no longer know what is being talked about.
C It depends on the contract; grammar does not decide it.
2. And in the version "The interlocking units, which failed the EMC test, must be reworked"? What CHANGES?
A Nothing — in English the comma is only a reading pause.
B It sounds more formal, but the meaning is the same.
C EVERYTHING: with the two commas the clause stops defining and starts ADDING a fact about ALL of them. The sentence now states that all forty failed — which is false, and it will be used against whoever wrote it.
3. "The report issued on 14 July does not cover the grounding." What structure is this?
A A REDUCED relative: which was has dropped out and the PAST participle is left, with a PASSIVE meaning — the report THAT WAS issued. This is how every technical standard is written.
B A mistake: the main verb is missing.
C An active -ing form: the report is issuing something.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "Please confirm the standard to that the design was certified."
B "Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified."
C "Please confirm the standard which the design was certified to it."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Relative Clauses & Precision

GRAMMAR

How the comma decides the scope — and why one sentence, with it and without it, commits the supplier to six units or to forty.

StructureWhat it doesExample
DEFINING relative
no commas
(that / which / who)		It DEFINES: it says WHICH ones. Take the clause out and you no longer know what is being talked about. This is the clause that sets the SCOPE of the work — and scope is money."The six units that failed the EMC test must be reworked." — those six, and no others.
NON-DEFINING relative
between two commas
(NEVER that)		It ADDS a fact about ALL of them. Take the clause out and the sentence still stands. After a comma, only which (things) or who (people)."The units, which failed the test, must be reworked." — ALL forty failed.
REDUCED relative
past participle		which was / that were drops out when the meaning is PASSIVE. It is the compression that every technical standard on his shelf is built on."the report which was issued on 14 July" → "the report issued on 14 July"
REDUCED relative
-ing		which is / that are drops out when the meaning is ACTIVE: the unit is DOING it, not receiving it."the units which are radiating above the limit" → "the units radiating above the limit"
Preposition + whichThe formal register of the RFI and of the specification. The preposition comes BEFORE which — and that can never follow a preposition."the standard to which the design was certified" · "the conditions under which the test was performed"
Why it mattersSix units failed. All forty share the same grounding. "The units that failed" commits the supplier to six. "The units, which failed," states that all forty failed — which is false, and their lawyer will point it out on the first reading. And "the grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified" gets all forty for the right reason. The grammar here is not decoration: it is the scope of the contract.

Watch out (three habits carried over from Portuguese): (1) "The RFI that we submitted it on Monday" — the object pronoun is NOT repeated in English: that is already the object; (2) "the configuration, that is common to all forty" — after a comma, that is impossible: use which; (3) "the cable installing last week" — the cable is not installing anything: it WAS installed, so it is installed (past participle). One form covers both cases in your first language; English needs two.

RFI collocations: submit an RFI (never "send"), respond to technical queries (never "answer to"), verify compliance with the specs (never "compliance TO"), ensure interoperability, validate the design, exceed the emission limit, implement a workaround, fall outside the scope, meet the acceptance criteria.

The question to ask before you hit send: "Can this sentence be read in a second way that costs me money?" If it can, it is not ready. Ambiguity is not confusion: an ambiguous sentence looks completely clear — and that is precisely why it gets through.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The six interlocking units the EMC test must be reworked at the supplier's cost."
"The grounding configuration, , must be re-verified."
"The report on 14 July does not cover the grounding configuration."
"The units above the limit are the six on the northbound platform."
"Please confirm the standard the design was certified."
"Please spell out the conditions the EMC test was performed."

Stage 2: Put the RFI in Order

Order

Put the steps of the RFI in the correct order — from locking the scope down to the date on which the workaround dies.

?Close the workaround: the ferrite filters are accepted for the trial run only, and not for the handover.
?Add the fact about all forty: the grounding configuration, which is common to the whole batch, has not been verified as installed.
?Compress the evidence: the report issued on 14 July does not cover the configuration.
?Ask formally: please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
?Restrict the scope: the six interlocking units that failed the EMC test are to be reworked.
?Ask formally again: please spell out the conditions under which the test was performed.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Notice the PAUSE around the commas in the second one: in spoken English a non-defining clause can be HEARD — there is a pause on either side of it, and that pause is what tells the other side you are talking about all forty units. And say in-ter-op-er-a-BIL-i-ty, with the stress on the fifth syllable.

The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked at the supplier's cost.
The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified.
The report issued on 14 July does not cover the grounding configuration.
Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
The workaround that your team devised does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the best answer for every real moment of the technical clarification call — including the moment the supplier argues that thirty-four passes prove the design, or offers a temporary fix as if it were the solution.

You want the supplier to rework ONLY the six units that failed. You write:
A "The interlocking units, which failed the EMC test, must be reworked at your cost."
B "The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test on 14 July must be reworked at your cost."
C "Please rework the units with the interference problem as soon as possible."
You want to put on record that the grounding is the SAME on all forty — without stating that all forty failed:
A "The forty units, which failed the EMC test, share the same grounding configuration."
B "The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, has not been verified in the installed condition."
C "All the units have a grounding problem that needs to be looked at."
Ingrid says: "Thirty-four units passed, so the design is correct." The strongest reply is:
A "That is a reasonable hypothesis, and it may well be right. But thirty-four units passing a test does not demonstrate a design — and two of them passed by almost nothing."
B "That is not true and you know it."
C "All right. If thirty-four passed, let us close the design question."
She offers ferrite filters "that fix it in a week". You answer:
A "Perfect. If the emissions come down, we can consider it closed."
B "I will take the filters for the trial run. I will not take them for the handover, because a workaround does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover."
C "We do not accept workarounds under any circumstances."
You need to know HOW they ran the test (on the bench or as installed). You ask:
A "How did you do the test exactly? Can you explain it to me?"
B "Please spell out the conditions under which the EMC test was performed, and confirm the standard to which the design was certified."
C "The conditions which you tested the design in them are not clear."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and with no stopping to correct yourself. Tone: precise about the scope, honest about what is still unknown, and impossible to reinterpret.

You are opening the clarification call on RFI-114. In ninety seconds: restrict the scope to the six interlocking units that failed the EMC test on 14 July, add the fact about the grounding configuration that is common to all forty without claiming that all forty failed, refuse the argument that thirty-four passes prove the design, accept the ferrite filters for the trial run only and give them an end date, and close with the two questions you need in writing -- the standard to which the design was certified, and the conditions under which the test was performed.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1The six interlocking units that failed the EMC test must be reworked at the supplier's cost.
2The grounding configuration, which is common to all forty units, must be re-verified.
3Please confirm the standard to which the design was certified.
4The workaround that your team devised does not meet the acceptance criteria that apply at handover.
5Until we pin down the source of the interference, any fix is a guess.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

Say That Again -- The Commissioning Call at Full Speed

A British project director, forty minutes and a commissioning schedule at native speed: how to FORECAST with a date attached (what will be DONE vs. what will be HAPPENING), where the tense hides in fast speech (the auxiliary collapses, the participle survives), and the three sentences that stop the call without losing face. Key words: integration test, site readiness, look-ahead schedule, open action, outstanding item, provisional acceptance, final acceptance, defects liability period, ramp-up, interface management, dry run, to phase out, to factor in, to run something by someone. Structure: future perfect and future continuous (By the twelfth we will have confirmed... / This time next week we will be running...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 8 you wrote the question with all the time in the world. Now the last phase of the project arrives SPOKEN, fast, and at somebody else's pace. These are the words of the final stretch: who accepts, when, and what is still open. Listen to each term and read the example.

Integration test -- the test that proves the separate systems talk to each other -- not that each one works alone
"The integration test between the interlocking and the control center starts on the fifteenth."
Site readiness -- whether the place itself -- power, access, safety -- can actually receive the equipment and the crews
"I will confirm site readiness on the twelfth, once the power supply is energized."
Look-ahead schedule -- the short window of the plan: the next three weeks, activity by activity, in detail
"The three-week look-ahead schedule shows the integration test starting on the fifteenth."
Open action -- a task with a name on it and a date on it, and it is not closed yet
"By Friday we will have closed every open action from the last call."
Outstanding item -- something that was agreed, is still not done, and is still on the list at the end of the meeting
"Three outstanding items remain, and none of them can wait until the ramp-up."
Provisional acceptance -- the client takes the system into service -- with a list of defects still attached to it
"Provisional acceptance is planned for the end of the third quarter."
Final acceptance -- nothing is left open, the warranty period is over, and the last payment is released
"Final acceptance will not be granted until every outstanding item is closed."
Defects liability period -- the window after handover in which the supplier still fixes, at its own cost, whatever fails
"The defects liability period runs for twelve months between provisional and final acceptance."
Ramp-up -- the gradual climb from the first test run to full service, week by week
"By the time the ramp-up begins in November, we will have trained every operator."
Interface management -- making two suppliers' systems meet without a gap -- and owning the gap when there is one
"Interface management between signaling and rolling stock sits with me."
Dry run -- a full rehearsal of the real operation, with nothing at stake and everybody watching
"This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure."
To phase out -- to remove something gradually, on a planned date, until it is gone for good
"The manual workarounds will be phased out before provisional acceptance, not after."
To factor in -- to include something in the calculation BEFORE you commit to the date
"Have you factored in the night-shift restrictions on the site?"
To run something by someone -- to repeat or check something with somebody, so that they can follow it or approve it
"Could you run the acceptance dates by me one more time?"

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Integration test
Site readiness
Look-ahead schedule
Open action
Outstanding item
Provisional acceptance
Final acceptance
Defects liability period
Ramp-up
Interface management
Dry run
To phase out
To factor in
To run something by someone

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The call starts at eight and Fiona Hartley, the project director in London, has another one at half past. She goes straight in. Where are we on site readiness? Because by the twelfth she needs the power supply energized, or the integration test does not happen. She speaks the way people speak when they are late: the auxiliaries collapse, will have becomes will'uv, will be almost disappears, and the dates arrive in the middle of long sentences with no emphasis at all.

So João Guilherme does the thing that feels wrong and is right: he stops her. I want to make sure I am following — the deadline you are referring to is the twelfth, and that is for site readiness, not for the test itself. She says "Correct" and carries on. Five seconds. Then he forecasts, and every sentence carries a date. By the fifteenth we will have energized the power supply and closed the two open actions on the cable route. We will be working nights from the eighth to hold that date. This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure. By the time the ramp-up starts in November, every operator will have been trained.

Then comes the part that decides the money. Provisional acceptance at the end of the third quarter; final acceptance twelve months later, with the defects liability period running in between. And the manual workarounds, Fiona says, get phased out BEFORE provisional acceptance, not after — she has seen too many projects where the temporary fix quietly becomes the design. João asks her to run the acceptance dates by him one more time, because he wants to factor them in before he sends the three-week look-ahead schedule. And he closes the call the way a call has to be closed: Just to confirm — are you saying that no workaround survives provisional acceptance? Then by the thirtieth we will have cleared every outstanding item on that list. He did not understand every word she said. He understood every date — and that is the entire job.

1. "By the fifteenth we will have energized the power supply." What exactly does this structure promise?
A That the energization will HAPPEN on the fifteenth.
B That the energization will be COMPLETE BEFORE the fifteenth — will have + past participle is the tense of the DEADLINE, and the deadline is what the contract reads.
C That the energization begins on the fifteenth and carries on after that.
2. "This time next week we will be running a dry run." Why will be running and not will run?
A Because will be running sounds more formal.
B Because the action will be IN PROGRESS at that moment — neither finished nor starting. Will run would sound like a decision taken on the spot.
C Because English does not allow the simple future after next week.
3. "By the time the ramp-up starts, the workarounds will have been phased out." Why starts and not will start?
A Because after by the time / when / before / as soon as, English writes the future in the PRESENT. "By the time it will start" does not exist.
B Because the ramp-up has already begun.
C Because start is an irregular verb.
4. Fiona drops the new date in the MIDDLE of a long sentence, with no emphasis at all. What is the professional reaction?
A Note down whatever you managed to catch and check it later by email, so you do not look slow.
B Stop her and confirm it out loud: "Just to confirm — are you saying that provisional acceptance has moved to the twenty-third?" Five seconds there save three weeks of work toward the wrong date.
C Carry on with the call and ask her to speak more slowly next time.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Future Perfect & Future Continuous

GRAMMAR

The two tenses a project is run with — and why they almost disappear in fast speech.

StructureWhat it promisesOn the call
will have + past participle
future perfect		DONE before that point in the future. This is the tense of the DEADLINE — the one the contract reads, and the one somebody can hold you to."By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness."
will be + verb -ing
future continuous		IN PROGRESS at that moment: not finished, not starting — happening. The simple future would sound like a decision taken on the spot."This time next week we will be running the dry run."
will have been + verb -ing
future perfect continuous		It measures the DURATION built up to that point. This is the one that justifies a cost or an extension of time."By September the crews will have been working nights for six weeks."
by vs. untilBY = the deadline (no later than). UNTIL = the whole duration. Portuguese covers both with a single word, which is why Brazilian engineers write "until Friday we will have finished" — a sentence that means nothing in English."By Friday" (deadline) · "We will be testing until Friday" (duration)
by the time + PRESENTAfter by the time / when / before / as soon as / until, the future is written in the PRESENT. "By the time the ramp-up will start" DOES NOT exist."By the time the ramp-up starts, the workarounds will have been phased out."
How it SOUNDSNobody says these tenses the way they are written. will have becomes /wɪləv/ ("will'uv"), we will have becomes "we'll've", and will be almost vanishes. The tense itself does NOT vanish with it: it survives in the PARTICIPLE, at the end of the sentence (...we'll've CONFIRMED it). Stop hunting for the auxiliary you were taught at school: listen for the LAST word.

Watch out (three classic mistakes): (1) "Until the twelfth we will have confirmed..." — it is BY the twelfth: until is duration, by is a deadline; (2) "By the time the test will start..." — after by the time you use the PRESENT: starts; (3) "At ten we will run the test" when you mean that the test will be under way — the right form is we will be running: the simple future sounds like a decision made at that very instant.

Commissioning collocations: confirm site readiness (never "guarantee") · close an open action (never "finish") · clear the outstanding items · grant provisional acceptance (the client GRANTS it; the supplier OBTAINS it) · phase out a workaround · factor in a constraint (NEVER "consider in") · run a dry run · manage the interfaces.

Clarifying under pressure (learn these three by heart): "Could you run that by me one more time?" · "Just to confirm — are you saying that...?" · "I want to make sure I'm following: the deadline you're referring to is..." — none of them is an apology. They are what a native speaker says when a date gets past him, and they cost five seconds.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — and notice how the auxiliary almost disappears.

"By the twelfth we site readiness in writing."
"This time next week we a dry run of the failover procedure."
"By the time the ramp-up , the manual workarounds will have been phased out."
"By September the crews nights for six weeks."
"I need to the night-shift restrictions before I commit to that date."
"Could you one more time?"

Stage 2: Put the Conference Call in Order

Order

Put in the right order the steps of somebody who never loses a date on a fast call — from catching the information she buried to committing with the right tense.

?Catch the date she buried in the middle of the sentence: provisional acceptance has moved to the twenty-third.
?Say what will be happening, not finished: "We will be working nights from the eighth to hold that date."
?Factor in what she cannot see: the night-shift restrictions on site.
?Ask her to repeat what you did not catch: "Could you run the acceptance dates by me one more time?"
?Stop her and confirm it out loud: "Just to confirm -- are you saying that provisional acceptance is now the twenty-third?"
?Commit with the deadline tense: "By the twenty-third we will have closed every outstanding item."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Say it fast, running the words together: will have becomes "will'uv" and will be almost vanishes — producing the reduced form is what trains your ear to catch it. Watch the stress: pro-VI-sion-al, li-a-BI-li-ty, RED-i-ness (short, as in "ready").

By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness in writing.
This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure.
By the time the ramp-up starts, the manual workarounds will have been phased out.
Could you run that by me one more time?
I want to make sure I'm following: the deadline you're referring to is the twelfth, not the fifteenth.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the call — including the one where she buries the new date in the middle of a long sentence.

Fiona opens the call at full speed: "Where are we on site readiness? By the twelfth I need that power supply energized." You reply:
A "I want to make sure I'm following -- the deadline you're referring to is the twelfth, and that is for site readiness, not for the integration test itself. Is that right?"
B "Sorry, could you speak more slowly, please? My English is not very good."
C "Yes, yes, no problem, everything is on track."
She asks for the status. The strongest answer is:
A "We are working on it and I think we will finish before the test."
B "By the fifteenth we will have energized the power supply and closed the two open actions on the cable route, and we will be working nights from the eighth to hold that date."
C "The site will be ready, do not worry about that."
Halfway through a long sentence she says that provisional acceptance has moved to the twenty-third. You:
A Write it down and check it later against her email, so as not to interrupt her.
B Stop her: "Just to confirm -- are you saying that provisional acceptance has moved to the twenty-third? Then by the twenty-third we will have cleared every outstanding item."
C Assume you misheard the date and carry on with the call as usual.
"Let us just keep the manual workarounds until final acceptance, to be safe." You push back:
A "The workarounds are phased out before provisional acceptance, not after. A temporary fix with no end date quietly becomes the design, and the operator pays to maintain it for thirty years."
B "All right, if that is easier for your team, we can keep them."
C "I do not have the authority to decide that."
She asks whether you still hold the date, even with the night-shift restriction on site. You say:
A "Yes, of course, we will make it work somehow."
B "I need to factor in the night-shift restrictions before I commit to that date. You will have the three-week look-ahead schedule tonight, and the date will be in it."
C "That is impossible. Nobody can work with these restrictions."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. Tone: fast enough to keep up, precise enough never to promise the wrong thing.

You are on the commissioning call with the project director in London, and she is running the schedule at full speed. In two minutes: give her the status of site readiness with a date in front of every sentence, say what will have been finished by the twelfth and what your crews will be doing in between, catch the acceptance date she gives you and confirm it out loud, refuse to carry the manual workarounds past provisional acceptance, and close by summarizing what each side will have done by the end of the month.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness in writing.
2This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure.
3Could you run that by me one more time?
4Just to confirm — are you saying that the workarounds come out before provisional acceptance?
5I want to make sure I'm following: the deadline you're referring to is the twelfth, not the fifteenth.
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

The Interview Room -- Performing Under Pressure

The interview in Brussels went badly. This is the reverse-engineering: the STAR method for behavioral questions, and the grammar that narrates your evolution — used to / be used to / get used to. Key words: STAR method, behavioral question, situational question, transferable skills, gap analysis, selling point, culture fit, notice period, relocation, to cut to the chase, to read between the lines, to think on your feet, to come across as, to sell yourself short. Structure: used to + base verb (finished past) vs. be / get used to + -ing (accustomed), plus would rather / had better / may as well for positioning.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Last week you decoded native speech at full speed. Here you walk into the room. These are the words a hiring panel speaks: how they ask, what they judge, and the honest word for what you must never do to yourself. Listen to each term and read the example.

STAR method -- a four-part way to structure an interview answer: Situation, Task, Action, Result
"When they ask for an example, I answer with the STAR method so nothing is missing."
Behavioral question -- a question that asks what you actually did in a real past situation ("Tell me about a time...")
"A behavioral question wants a story, not a philosophy."
Situational question -- a question about a hypothetical case ("What would you do if...")
"A situational question tests judgment; a behavioral one tests track record."
Transferable skills -- abilities you built in one role that carry over to a different job or industry
"They hire for transferable skills, not for one specific platform."
Gap analysis -- comparing where you are now with where the role needs you to be, and naming the difference
"Before the interview I did a gap analysis: what the role wants, and what I still have to prove."
Selling point -- the specific strength that makes you the right person, stated so the panel remembers it
"My selling point is the international interface: I have run it in three languages."
Culture fit -- how well the way you work matches the way the company works
"Between two equal engineers, culture fit decides who gets the offer."
Notice period -- the time you must keep working for your current employer after you resign
"My notice period is thirty days, so I could start early next quarter."
Relocation -- moving to another city or country for a job
"The role is in Zurich, and relocation support is provided for the family."
To cut to the chase -- to skip the preamble and get straight to the point that matters
"Let me cut to the chase: my strongest area is the international interface."
To read between the lines -- to understand the real meaning that is implied but not stated
"Read between the lines: they care more about range than about their exact product."
To think on your feet -- to respond well and quickly to something you did not prepare for
"A panel is designed to make you think on your feet, so a short pause is allowed."
To come across as -- to give a certain impression to the person judging you
"A calm pause makes you come across as senior, not slow."
To sell yourself short -- to describe yourself as less capable than you really are
"Do not sell yourself short: you led the interface, so say you led it."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

STAR method
Behavioral question
Situational question
Transferable skills
Gap analysis
Selling point
Culture fit
Notice period
Relocation
To cut to the chase
To read between the lines
To think on your feet
To come across as
To sell yourself short

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

An interview in Brussels went badly in March, and João Guilherme has decided to reverse-engineer it. A recruiter, Claire Mercier, has now invited him to a competency-based panel for a Senior Signaling Engineer role in Zurich, and this time he is preparing the version of himself that walks out in control. He starts with a gap analysis: what the role needs, and what he still has to prove — and he reads between the lines of the posting, which values transferable skills and culture fit over any single product.

The grammar of the interview is small, and it decides everything. Watch the word right after used to. "I used to work on electrification" — base verb — is a finished past habit: where he came from. "I'm used to managing suppliers in three time zones" — the -ing — is a state that is already normal for him. And "I got used to reading CENELEC standards in English" is the process in between: strange, then normal, over time. Same three words, three meanings, and a panel hears the difference. Together they narrate an arc — from, through, to — which is exactly what a hiring manager wants.

So he builds his answers. When a behavioral question lands ("Tell me about a time you managed a supplier conflict"), he uses the STAR method: he cuts to the chase, names the story first, then runs Situation, Task, Action, Result — because the Result is the sentence that buys the job. When a situational question lands ("What would you do if..."), he thinks on his feet and takes the short pause that makes him come across as senior. He positions himself without arrogance: "I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills", "I had better be specific here", "we may as well start with the hardest project I have run". And he refuses his oldest habit: he does not sell himself short. He owned the interface, so he says he owned it — and he closes the room on relocation and his notice period without hedging, landing on the one selling point the panel will remember.

1. "I used to work on electrification." What does this tell the panel?
A That he still works on electrification today.
B That it is a FINISHED past habit — used to + base verb means it was true before and is over now. It is where he came FROM.
C That he is currently learning electrification.
2. And "I'm used to managing suppliers in three time zones"? What CHANGES?
A Nothing — it means the same as "I used to manage".
B It is a mistake; after "used to" the verb should be the base form.
C EVERYTHING: be used to + -ING is a STATE that is already normal for him NOW. "I used to manage" = finished; "I'm used to managing" = normal today.
3. When a behavioral question lands, what is the best move?
A Cut to the chase, name the story first, and run STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result — because the Result is what buys the job.
B Give your general philosophy of conflict, without a specific example.
C Say you cannot remember a specific time and describe your personality instead.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "I would rather to give you one real result than a list of skills."
B "I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills."
C "I am used to give you one real result than a list of skills."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Used To / Be Used To / Get Used To

GRAMMAR

One little word after "used to" decides the meaning — a base verb for a finished past, an -ing for what is normal now.

StructureMeaningExample
used to + base verbA past habit or state that is now FINISHED — where you came from. Negative/question drop the -d: "I didn't use to...", "Did you use to...?""I used to work on electrification." — I did, and I stopped.
be used to + -ing / nounA STATE: it is already normal for you now. NOT the base verb — the -ing."I'm used to managing suppliers." — normal for me now.
get used to + -ing / nounThe PROCESS of becoming accustomed — from strange to normal, over time."I got used to reading CENELEC in English." — it became normal.
would rather / had better / may as well
+ base verb (no "to")		would rather = preference. had better = strong advice, almost a warning. may as well = no better option, so let's."I would rather give a result." · "I had better be specific." · "We may as well start with the hardest one."
Why it mattersThese structures narrate your evolution in one breath: "I used to work only in Portuguese; I got used to running calls in English; now I'm used to managing the interface across three countries." That arc — from, through, to — is exactly what a panel wants, and the verb form is the only thing that makes it come out right.

Watch out (habits carried over from Portuguese): (1) "I am used to manage" — after be/get used to the verb takes -ing: managing. Your first language uses one form for both, which tempts you into the base verb; English needs the -ing; (2) "I used to working" — a past habit takes the base verb: used to work; (3) "I would rather to give" — would rather / had better / may as well take the base verb with no "to".

Interview collocations: value transferable skills, come across as confident (never "come across confident"), serve / work a notice period, be shortlisted for a role, provide relocation support, cut to the chase, answer a behavioral question, think on your feet.

The question to ask before every answer: "Did I give them the Result?" Most candidates tell the Situation and stop. The STAR answer without the R is a story with no ending — and it is the R that buys the job.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I on electrification before I specialized in signaling."
"I'm suppliers across three time zones."
"It took time, but I CENELEC standards in English."
"I give you one real result than a list of skills."
"Let me give you a concrete example, using the ."
"A calm pause makes you senior, not slow."

Stage 2: Build the Interview in Order

Order

Put the moves of a strong panel answer in order — from the opening arc to closing the room on relocation.

?Open with the arc: I used to work on electrification, and I got used to owning the interface.
?Position yourself: I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills.
?Close the room: I'm open to relocation, and my notice period is thirty days.
?State what is normal now: I'm used to running technical calls in English under pressure.
?Run the STAR structure: Situation, Task, Action, and above all the Result.
?When the behavioral question lands, cut to the chase and name the story first.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Notice the -ing after "used to managing", and keep "trans-FER-a-ble" and "re-lo-CA-tion" stressed on the right syllable.

I used to work on electrification, and I got used to owning the international interface.
I'm used to managing suppliers across three time zones on a live commissioning.
Let me give you a concrete example, using the STAR method.
I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills.
I'm open to relocation, and my notice period is thirty days.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the best answer for every real moment of the panel — the opening, the behavioral question, the platform gap, the unplanned question, and the close on relocation.

The panel asks "Tell me about yourself." The strongest opening is:
A "I only did some international work, nothing major, my English is not so good."
B "I'm a signaling engineer at Motiva. I used to work on electrification, and over three years I got used to owning the interface, so now I'm used to running it in English."
C "I'm experienced, communicative and good with international teams."
A behavioral question lands: "Tell me about a time you managed a supplier conflict." You:
A Give your general opinion on how conflicts should be handled.
B Cut to the chase and run STAR: six units failed the EMC test (S), my task was to keep the design question open (T), I put the shared design on record (A), and all forty were re-verified at their cost (R).
C Say you would rather not talk about conflicts.
You have never worked on their exact platform. The best framing is:
A "So maybe I am not really qualified for this role."
B "You hire for range, not one platform. My transferable skills carry over, and I got used to new standards fast at Motiva."
C "I used to working on other platforms, so it is fine."
They ask something you did not prepare. You want to think on your feet without looking lost. You say:
A "I don't know, sorry."
B "That is a good question — let me think for a second, because I would rather give you a real example than a general answer."
C You stay completely silent and hope they move on.
Claire asks about relocation and your notice period. You answer:
A "I'm open to relocation — I got used to being deployed to site in 2023 — and my notice period is thirty days, so I could start early next quarter."
B "I am not sure, maybe I could move, I would have to think about it."
C "My notice period is a problem, so perhaps this will not work."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and with no stopping to correct yourself. Tone: precise, evolving, and never smaller than you really are.

You have ninety seconds to answer "Tell me about yourself" for a Senior Signaling Engineer role in Zurich. Give the arc of your career using used to / got used to / am used to, land on your selling point (the international interface), and do not sell yourself short. Then handle one behavioral question in full STAR -- name the story first, and finish with the Result.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1I used to work on electrification, and I got used to owning the international interface.
2I'm used to running technical calls in English, under real schedule pressure.
3Let me give you a concrete example, using the STAR method.
4That is a good question -- let me think for a second.
5I would rather give you one real result than a list of skills.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

Milestone Review -- Consolidating What You Know

Halfway through the program. Nothing is new today and everything is on the table at once: the structures you already own (third conditional, cleft, inversion, causative, future perfect) in a single post-inspection debrief call with a defensive French supplier — plus the one new bridge, the MIXED CONDITIONAL, that ties a mistake in the past to a problem you are living now. Key words: leverage, scope creep, inspection protocol, status update, the elephant in the room, deliverable, milestone, stakeholder, compliance, commissioning, handover, procurement, to escalate, sign-off. Structure (new): mixed conditionals (If they had followed the protocol, we wouldn't be behind now).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

A milestone review is a consolidation, so most of these words are the spine of the whole program — and a few are new: the ones that name a problem instead of hiding it. Listen to each term and read the example.

Leverage -- an advantage you can actually use to get a better outcome in a negotiation
"The signed inspection protocol is our leverage -- they agreed to the criteria in writing."
Scope creep -- the slow, unapproved growth of a project beyond what was originally agreed
"Three extra tests appeared with no change order -- that is scope creep, and we are not paying for it."
Inspection protocol -- the agreed, written procedure for how an inspection is carried out and documented
"Both sides signed the inspection protocol before the factory acceptance test."
Status update -- a short, regular report on where every open item stands right now
"I send a written status update every Friday, whether there is good news in it or not."
The elephant in the room -- the obvious problem everyone can see but nobody wants to name
"Let me name the elephant in the room: the delay started on your side, not ours."
Deliverable -- a concrete item the project must produce and hand over
"The test report is a contractual deliverable, not a favor, and it is three weeks late."
Milestone -- a fixed checkpoint that marks real, verifiable progress
"We are at the halfway milestone, and on paper we are eight weeks behind."
Stakeholder -- anyone with a real interest in how the project turns out
"Every stakeholder on this package is watching the halfway milestone, so the number has to be honest."
Compliance -- meeting the rules, standards and contract to the letter
"On compliance we are exposed, so I would rather be slow and right than fast and wrong."
Commissioning -- the phase of testing and proving a system before it goes into service
"If the retest fails, commissioning slips, and the recovery plan moves with it."
Handover -- the formal transfer of a finished system, with its documents, to the client
"If those checks had been run in February, the handover would still be on its original date."
Procurement -- the process of sourcing, ordering and buying equipment and services
"Our procurement team released the cable schedule late, and I am going to own that in the room."
To escalate -- to raise an issue to a higher authority when it cannot be solved locally
"I will escalate to my manager only if you refuse to retest the three failed checks."
Sign-off -- the formal approval that closes an item or a phase
"We will have the results independently verified before any sign-off on this package."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Leverage
Scope creep
Inspection protocol
Status update
The elephant in the room
Deliverable
Milestone
Stakeholder
Compliance
Commissioning
Handover
Procurement
To escalate
Sign-off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Halfway through the program, and the numbers are not comfortable. The signaling package is eight weeks behind at the M6 milestone. Both sides signed the inspection protocol in February, and it lists three checks as mandatory hold points. Camille Laurent, the project manager for the French supplier, ran the factory test with only two of them and logged the result as a pass. That is João's leverage, and it is in writing.

She opens on the offensive: the whole delay, she says, is Motiva's, because the cable schedule arrived late. So João does the thing that takes nerve — he names the elephant in the room before she can use it. Our procurement team released the cable schedule late, and I own that. Then he turns. If your team had run the three checks, we wouldn't be renegotiating this milestone now. Notice the tense: the cause is in the past, the result is happening today — that is the mixed conditional, and it is the one new structure of the lesson. It was the third deferred check that caused this, not the cable schedule alone, he adds, putting the spotlight exactly where it belongs.

Camille calls it a documentation point and refuses a retest. João holds the line on compliance: a deferred check is not a passed check, and he cannot grant sign-off on a hold point that was skipped. We are going to have the three units retested against the full protocol, at your cost. When she pushes, he lays out the deal without raising his voice: he owns the cable delay in writing, she retests at her cost, and by the end of the month they will have agreed a recovery plan. She gets a written status update every Friday until handover. If she refuses the retest, he will escalate — and neither of them wants that. He did not learn a new language for this call. He reached for one he already had.

1. "If your team had run the checks, we wouldn't be renegotiating this milestone now." Why wouldn't be and not wouldn't have been?
A Because wouldn't be is more polite in a negotiation.
B Because the cause is in the PAST but the result is in the PRESENT — that is a mixed conditional, and a present result takes would + base verb, not would have + participle.
C Because English does not allow have been after wouldn't.
2. "If I were an engineer who cut corners, I would have signed that off." What does the past-simple condition (were) do here?
A It describes a permanent truth about who he IS, used to explain a decision he made in the PAST.
B It refers to a specific moment last February.
C It is simply a more formal way of writing was.
3. "It was the third deferred check that caused this, not the cable schedule." What is this structure doing?
A It is hiding who is responsible, to keep the tone soft.
B It is a cleft sentence — it puts one specific cause under a spotlight and pushes the blame to the exact right place.
C It is a mixed conditional linking past and present.
4. Camille opens by blaming Motiva's late cable schedule for everything. Why does João name Motiva's own fault FIRST?
A To apologize and end the disagreement quickly.
B Because naming the elephant in the room himself takes the weapon out of her hand — once he owns his side, there is nothing left for her to attack, and the protocol carries the rest.
C Because the cable schedule is the real and only cause of the delay.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Mixed Conditionals & the Structures You Own

GRAMMAR

One new bridge (the mixed conditional) and five structures from earlier lessons, on one page.

StructureWhat it doesOn the call
if + past perfect, would + base verb
mixed conditional -- NEW today		A PAST cause with a result you feel in the PRESENT. This is the one new step: it takes the third conditional from Lesson 2 and lands the result in the room you are in now."If they had followed the protocol, we wouldn't be behind now."
if + past simple, would have + participle
mixed conditional -- the other direction		A PRESENT, permanent truth that explains a PAST decision. The condition is who you ARE; the result is what you DID."If I were an engineer who cut corners, I would have signed that off."
It was ... that / What ... is
cleft -- Lessons 5 & 8		Puts one fact under a spotlight and pushes the blame or the request to the exact right place."It was the missing checks that caused the delay, not the cable schedule."
Never / Not only + auxiliary + subject
inversion -- Lessons 4 & 7		Formal, emphatic, and it makes a supplier sit up. The auxiliary jumps in front of the subject."Never have we accepted a test that skipped a mandatory hold point."
have + object + past participle
causative -- Lesson 6		You arrange it; someone else does it; they pay. The language of an engineer who directs work."We are going to have the three units retested at your cost."
will have + past participle
future perfect -- Lesson 9		The tense of the deadline: done BEFORE a date, and the one a contract can hold you to."By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan."
wish (the near cousin)wish + past perfect = regret about the past ("I wish they had flagged it earlier"). wish + past simple = a present you want changed ("I wish I had more time on this"). wish + would = a complaint about someone's behavior ("I wish they would send a status update without being chased").

Watch out (two classic mistakes): (1) "If they had followed it, we wouldn't HAVE BEEN behind now" — when the result is now, drop the have been and say be; (2) "If I HAD BEEN an engineer who cut corners..." — when the condition is a permanent truth, use the past simple: were. Your third conditional is so strong it wants both halves in the past — the mixed conditional refuses.

The point of a milestone review: none of these six is meant to be new — five of them you already own. What today trains is the difference between recognizing a structure (you read it, you understand it) and using it (it comes out of your mouth, under pressure, without you reaching for it). Take one real fact and say it five ways.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"If your team had run the checks, we renegotiating this milestone now."
"If I an engineer who cut corners, I would have signed that off months ago."
" the three missing checks that caused the delay, not the cable schedule."
"We are going to the three units retested against the full protocol."
"By the end of the month we a recovery plan."
"I will this only if you refuse to retest the three checks."

Stage 2: Put the Debrief in Order

Order

Put in the right order the moves of an engineer who runs a defensive supplier through every structure he owns — from naming his own side's fault to closing with a dated commitment.

?Link the past to the present: if that check had been run, we wouldn't be renegotiating the milestone now.
?Hold the line on compliance: a deferred check is not a passed check, so I cannot grant sign-off on it.
?Direct the fix with the causative: we are going to have the three units retested at your cost.
?Anchor the position in the signed protocol: it was the third deferred check that caused this, not the cable schedule alone.
?Name the elephant in the room first: our procurement team released the cable schedule late, and I own that.
?Close with a dated commitment: by the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan and a weekly status update.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Say it as you would in the room — calm, unhurried, and precise. Watch the stress: LEV-er-age, com-PLY-ance, pro-CURE-ment.

Let me name the elephant in the room: part of this delay is ours, and I own it.
It was the missing checks that caused the delay, not the cable schedule.
If your team had run the checks, we wouldn't be renegotiating this milestone now.
We are going to have the three units retested against the full protocol, at your cost.
By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan, and you will have a status update every Friday.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the debrief call — including the shortcut she offers and the scope creep she slips in.

Camille opens: "This milestone is behind because Motiva released the cable schedule late. Let us be clear about that." The strongest reply is:
A "Let me name the elephant in the room: our procurement team was late, and I own that. But a deferred check is not a passed check, and the protocol we both signed says so."
B "That is not true. The delay is entirely on your side."
C "You are right, it is our fault. Let us move on."
She says only two of the three mandatory checks were run, and calls the third a "practical decision." You:
A Accept it, since the units are probably fine anyway.
B Reframe it on compliance: "A deferred check is not a passed check. On compliance I cannot sign off on a skipped hold point, so we are going to have the three units retested against the full protocol."
C Say you will check the protocol later and get back to her.
You want to connect the February mistake to today's delay without simply accusing her. You say:
A "Your team made a serious error and now we are all paying for it."
B "If that check had been run in February, we wouldn't be having this conversation now."
C "If that check would have been run, we would not have this problem."
She offers to log the third check as "closed by analysis" instead of re-running it. You push back:
A "That works for me, it saves everyone time."
B "Would that survive an audit? A skipped hold point closed on paper is exactly what a regulator looks for. We retest it, or I escalate."
C "I am not sure, let me ask my manager and decide next week."
She asks you to accept two extra tests "so we are aligned." There is no change order. You say:
A "Of course, if it keeps us aligned we can add them."
B "Two extra tests with no change order is scope creep, and I am not signing off on it. We run the protocol we agreed, nothing more and nothing less."
C "Let us split the difference and add just one."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. The point of a milestone review: reach for the structures, do not wait for them.

You are running the post-inspection debrief with Camille, the French supplier's project manager, and she is defensive about the delay. In two minutes: name Motiva's own fault first, anchor everything in the inspection protocol both sides signed, link the deferred check in February to the problem you are living now with a mixed conditional, direct the retest with the causative, refuse a compliance shortcut and any scope creep, and close with a dated commitment and a weekly status update.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Let me name the elephant in the room: part of this delay is ours, and I own it.
2It was the missing checks that caused the delay, not the cable schedule.
3If your team had run the checks, we wouldn't be renegotiating this milestone now.
4We are going to have the three units retested against the full protocol, at your cost.
5By the end of the month we will have agreed a recovery plan, and you will have a status update every Friday.
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

Navigating Conflict -- Pushing Back Without Burning Bridges

A German supplier missed three delivery milestones and now blames Motiva's late drawing approval. This is how you answer: assertive, not aggressive; conceding the true part and holding the line on the rest. Key words: assertive, to defuse, a sticking point, an impasse, common ground, to meet halfway, to hold someone accountable, remediation plan, to draw the line, to table a proposal, goodwill, to smooth things over. Structure: concession and contrast — although / even though / while / whereas + a clause, despite / in spite of + a noun or -ing, and however / nevertheless between two sentences.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Last week you ran the milestone review. Tonight the milestone is missed — by a supplier who is now blaming you. These are the words of a conflict you win without burning the bridge: how you stay firm, how you give ground, and how you keep the relationship. Listen to each term and read the example.

Assertive -- confident and direct without being aggressive or passive -- you say the hard thing clearly and respectfully
"Assertive is not aggressive: I stated our position plainly, but I never attacked the person."
To defuse -- to reduce the tension in a situation before it escalates into a fight
"When he got defensive, I defused it: 'I'm not blaming you, I'm reading the schedule.'"
A sticking point -- the single issue that is blocking an agreement while everything else is settled
"We agreed on the schedule; the only sticking point was who pays for the recovery shifts."
An impasse -- a deadlock where neither side will move and the discussion cannot go forward
"To break the impasse, I proposed we table the cost question and agree the delivery sequence first."
Common ground -- the points both sides already agree on, used as a starting place to rebuild the talk
"Before arguing about the cause, we found common ground: we both wanted Line 4 delivered."
To meet halfway -- to compromise so that each side gives up something to reach a deal
"I couldn't accept full blame, but I offered to meet them halfway on the schedule."
To hold someone accountable -- to make someone answer for what they were responsible for
"The plan holds both sides accountable: their slip, and our nine-day drawing delay."
Remediation plan -- an agreed plan of actions to fix a failure and recover the schedule
"We left the call with a remediation plan, not a winner and a loser."
To draw the line -- to set a firm limit that you will not cross, however much pressure you are under
"I conceded the drawing delay, but I drew the line at signing sole responsibility."
To table a proposal -- in American English, to postpone discussion of a proposal until a later point
"Let's table the penalty clause for now and agree the recovery sequence first."
Goodwill -- the trust and positive relationship two parties have built and want to protect
"A formal dispute would win the point and burn two years of goodwill."
To smooth things over -- to repair a relationship after friction or a disagreement
"The start was tense, but we smoothed things over and both signed the plan."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Assertive
To defuse
A sticking point
An impasse
Common ground
To meet halfway
To hold someone accountable
Remediation plan
To draw the line
To table a proposal
Goodwill
To smooth things over

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

A German supplier, GleisTech, has missed three delivery milestones on Line 4, and this morning a letter arrived claiming the whole delay was Motiva's fault: the technical drawings were approved nine days late. João Guilherme is the engineer who has to answer it — this afternoon, on a call. His manager's brief is exact: do not go in swinging, and do not roll over. The goal is not to win an argument; it is to leave with a remediation plan and a supplier who still wants the next contract. In other words, protect the goodwill.

The grammar of that call is the grammar of concession. Watch how a firm point is built on top of a fact you accept. "Although we were nine days late on the January revision, that does not explain a three-month slip." The although-clause gives ground; the main clause holds it. "While I accept the drawing was late, the delay after approval is yours to resolve." "Our records show March, whereas the baseline called for January." And when the connector takes no clause: "Despite the late approval, we kept the line moving" — despite + a noun, never despite we were late. Each sentence concedes something true, then draws a line exactly where the evidence draws it.

So João runs the call the assertive way, which is neither aggressive nor passive. He defuses the defensiveness — "I'm not saying you dropped the ball; I'm saying the schedule shows a gap" — and looks for common ground before arguing about cause. When they reach a sticking point over who pays for the recovery shifts, he does not let it become an impasse: he offers to meet them halfway on the schedule while he draws the line on sole responsibility. He tables the hardest clause so nobody has to lose face, keeps both sides accountable to a revised sequence, and smooths things over before the call ends — the point made, the bridge intact. "Despite the tension, I'd rather meet you halfway than lose a month to a dispute."

1. "Although we were nine days late on the January revision, that does not explain a three-month slip." What is the main point of the sentence?
A That Motiva caused the whole delay.
B That the nine-day delay is real but does NOT explain the rest — the although-clause concedes a fact, and the MAIN clause carries the weight.
C That the January revision was never late.
2. Which sentence is grammatically correct?
A "Despite we were late on the drawings, we kept the line moving."
B "Despite the late approval, we kept the line moving."
C "Despite of the late approval, we kept the line moving."
3. "Our records show delivery in March, whereas the baseline called for January." What does whereas do here?
A It draws a clean contrast between two facts placed side by side.
B It apologizes for the March delivery.
C It means 'because', giving the reason for the delay.
4. The manager says the goal of the call is to leave with a remediation plan and a supplier who still wants to work with Motiva. What is he protecting?
A The penalty clause.
B The goodwill — the working relationship, so pushing back must never become burning the bridge.
C His own promotion.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Concession & Contrast

GRAMMAR

Concede a true point, then hold your line. What changes is the grammar AFTER the connector — a clause, a noun, or a new sentence.

StructureWhat follows itExample
Although / Even though+ a FULL CLAUSE (subject + verb). Concede a fact, then make your real point in the main clause. Even though is the more emphatic version."Although we were late on one revision, that does not explain the slip."
While / Whereas+ a FULL CLAUSE. While = "although" or "at the same time as"; whereas draws a clean contrast between two facts."Our records show March, whereas the baseline called for January."
Despite / In spite of+ a NOUN or an -ing form — NEVER a full clause. "despite the delay", "despite being late" — not "despite we were late"."Despite the late approval, we kept the line moving."
However / NeverthelessJoin two SEPARATE sentences: full stop before, comma after. They pivot the reader from one sentence to the next."We accept the drawing delay. However, the schedule tells a different story."
Why it mattersConcession is how you push back without burning the bridge. You give ground on a small, true point — "although we were nine days late" — and that concession buys the credibility to hold firm on the big one. The engineer who admits nothing is believed on nothing. Conceding first is not weakness; it is the move that keeps the room open.

Watch out (habits carried over from Portuguese): (1) "Despite we were late..." — after despite / in spite of comes a NOUN or -ing, not a clause: "despite the delay", "despite being late". For a full clause, use although; (2) "Despite of the delay..." — it is despite (no "of") or in spite of (with "of"), never "despite of"; (3) "Although late, but we kept moving" — English uses although OR but, never both in the same sentence.

Conflict collocations: defuse a situation, reach an impasse, find common ground, meet someone halfway, hold someone accountable, draw the line, table a proposal, preserve goodwill, smooth things over, push back on a claim (not on a person).

The question to ask before you reply in a conflict: "What can I honestly concede first?" Find the true point you can give away, concede it with although, and only then draw your line. Concede, then contrast — every time.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
