Complete each sentence with the correct connector or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

" we were late on one revision, the rest of the delay began on their side."
" the tension, I'd rather meet them halfway than lose a month."
"Our records show March, the baseline called for January."
"I can commit to a joint plan. , I can't accept sole responsibility."
"Before arguing about the cause, we found ."
"I conceded the drawing delay, but I had to at sole responsibility."

Stage 2: Build the Conflict Call in Order

Order

Put the moves of an assertive conflict call in order — from conceding the true part to closing with the bridge intact.

?Close by smoothing things over: despite the tension, we leave with a remediation plan we both signed.
?Draw the line where the evidence draws it: the post-approval slip stays with your team.
?Open by conceding the true part: although we were nine days late, that does not explain the whole slip.
?Defuse the defensiveness: I'm not saying you dropped the ball; I'm saying the schedule shows a gap.
?Break the sticking point: table the cost clause for now and agree the delivery sequence first.
?Find common ground before arguing about cause: we both want Line 4 back on its critical path.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Keep "al-THOUGH" and "de-SPITE" stressed on the second syllable, and let "however" pivot with a small pause: "...delay. However, the schedule..."

Although we were nine days late on one revision, that does not explain a three-month slip.
I'm not saying you dropped the ball; I'm saying the schedule shows a gap.
Despite the late approval, we kept the installation line moving.
I can commit to a remediation plan. However, I can't accept sole responsibility.
Let's find the common ground first, then draw the line where the evidence draws it.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the best answer for every real moment of the conflict call — the blame, the defensiveness, the sticking point, the document, and the close.

The supplier opens by blaming your late drawings for the entire delay. Your strongest first move is:
A "That's completely wrong, the delay is entirely your fault."
B "Although we were nine days late on the January revision, and I accept that openly, the baseline schedule shows the rest of the delay began on your side."
C "You're right, it's all our fault, let's move on."
They get defensive and stop listening. To defuse it without giving up your position, you say:
A "You clearly dropped the ball on this one."
B "I'm not saying you dropped the ball -- I'm saying the schedule shows a gap, and I'd like us to close it together."
C "Fine, forget it, I'll escalate this to your director."
You reach a sticking point over who pays for the recovery shifts. To avoid an impasse, you:
A Refuse to discuss anything else until they admit fault.
B Offer to meet them halfway on the schedule while you draw the line on sole responsibility, and table the cost clause for later.
C Agree to pay everything just to end the call.
They ask you to sign a document that assigns Motiva sole responsibility. You protect Motiva and the relationship by saying:
A "I'll sign whatever ends this argument."
B "I can commit to a joint remediation plan; however, I can't sign a document that assigns us sole responsibility. Let's table that clause and agree the sequence first."
C "No, and I'm ending this call now."
The call was tense but productive. To close it and keep the goodwill, you say:
A "Despite a difficult start, I think we've smoothed things over -- I'll send the remediation plan today so we hold each other accountable."
B "Well, that was a waste of time."
C "We'll talk when you're ready to admit the truth."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and with no stopping to correct yourself. Tone: assertive, never aggressive — concede first, then hold the line.

You open the conflict call with GleisTech. In two minutes: concede the drawing delay openly using "although", defuse any defensiveness, find the common ground, and then draw the line on the post-approval slip using "whereas" or "however" — without ever attacking the person. Close by proposing a remediation plan that keeps both sides accountable and the goodwill intact.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Although we were late on one revision, that does not explain the whole delay.
2I'm not saying you dropped the ball; I'm saying the schedule shows a gap.
3Let's find the common ground first, then draw the line where the evidence draws it.
4I can commit to a remediation plan. However, I can't accept sole responsibility.
5Despite the tension, I'd rather meet you halfway than lose a month to a dispute.
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

Reading the Fine Print -- When the Standard Moved and Nobody Told You

Forty units certified to a revision that an amendment had already superseded: how to reconstruct the sequence of a non-conformance in the past perfect (what had happened FIRST, and how long each side had been working from the old revision), and how to pin down the fine print before you sign. Key words: provision, to stipulate, wording, loophole, to supersede, amendment, annex, normative reference, discrepancy, order of precedence, caveat, grey area, fine print, the devil is in the details. Structure: past perfect and past perfect continuous (By the time we caught it, the supplier had already shipped... / They had been manufacturing to the old revision for three months...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

In Lesson 12 you pushed back on a person. This week the conflict is with a DOCUMENT: a standard that had changed while nobody was reading the annex. These are the words of the fine print — who requires what, in which revision, and where the change was hidden. Listen to each term and read the example.

Provision -- a specific requirement written into a standard or a contract
"The provision on grounding is in Annex D, not in the main clause."
To stipulate -- to state a requirement clearly and formally in a written document
"The contract stipulates the standard in its current revision."
Wording -- the exact words of a clause, on which its whole meaning depends
"One word changed in the wording, and the obligation changed with it."
Loophole -- a gap in the wording that lets one side avoid an obligation
"They read the old annex as a loophole; I read it as an oversight."
To supersede -- when a newer revision replaces and cancels an earlier one
"Revision D superseded revision C in December."
Amendment -- a formal, documented change to a standard or a contract
"The amendment rewrote one provision and left the rest untouched."
Annex -- an extra section at the back of a standard, where the detailed requirements live
"Everybody reads the main clause; nobody reads Annex D."
Normative reference -- another standard that a standard makes mandatory by citing it
"The normative reference points to a document that had also been revised."
Discrepancy -- a conflict between two documents that are supposed to agree
"The discrepancy was between the certificate and the current revision."
Order of precedence -- the rule that decides which document wins when they conflict
"The order of precedence puts the contract above the supplier's data sheet."
Caveat -- a warning or condition attached to a statement or an approval
"I will approve it, with one caveat: subject to the amended annex."
Grey area -- a point the wording does not clearly cover
"Whose responsibility the revision check was is a grey area."
Fine print -- the detailed conditions, easy to miss, that carry the real obligations
"The fine print is where the deadline and the penalty actually live."
The devil is in the details -- a saying: the small details are where the real problems hide
"The clause looked clean, but the devil is in the details."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Provision
To stipulate
Wording
Loophole
To supersede
Amendment
Annex
Normative reference
Discrepancy
Order of precedence
Caveat
Grey area
Fine print
The devil is in the details

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The purchase order reached the German supplier in January, and at that moment revision C of the signaling standard was current, so the axle counters were certified to it. What nobody knew was that a committee amendment had already been approved in December, and that amendment had superseded revision C and rewritten a single provision in Annex D. It was not published on the portal until March. So by the time it appeared, the supplier had been manufacturing to the old revision for almost three months, and had already shipped the first batch.

The discrepancy only surfaced in April, when João Guilherme's inspector opened Annex D at the factory acceptance test. By then, both sides had been working from a document that no longer applied. The contract had stipulated the standard "in its current revision", and the wording mattered: current meant current on the day of delivery, and by February the current revision had already changed. Nobody on either side had checked the portal, and nobody had flagged it. It was not a loophole somebody had exploited; it was a grey area both teams had fallen into, because the main clause had stayed the same and neither had opened the annex.

So João did the thing that settles a dispute: he reconstructed the sequence, event by event, in the past. He established what had happened first, how long each side had been working from the superseded revision, and what neither party had done. Then he applied the order of precedence — the contract governs, and the contract pointed at a standard that had moved — and proposed a caveat: accept nothing until the provision is spelled out and the annex reference is fixed. He did not win by assigning blame. He won by putting the events in order, because once the timeline was clear, the liability stopped being an argument and became a date.

1. "By the time the discrepancy surfaced, the supplier had already shipped the first batch." What does had shipped tell you?
A That the shipping and the discovery happened at the same time.
B That the shipping came FIRST — had + past participle marks the earlier of two past events, and in a dispute that order is the liability.
C That the shipping had not happened yet when the discrepancy surfaced.
2. "They had been manufacturing to the old revision for three months." Why had been manufacturing and not were manufacturing?
A Because had been manufacturing sounds more formal.
B Because it measures a DURATION that had built up to a past point — the plain past continuous cannot show that the three months came before the amendment appeared.
C Because English does not allow the past continuous with for three months.
3. The contract stipulated the standard "in its current revision". Why did the certificate still not comply?
A Because the current revision had changed before delivery — an amendment had superseded revision C by the shipping date, so the certificate pointed at a revision that no longer applied.
B Because the contract had named revision D specifically.
C Because the supplier had never certified the units at all.
4. Why does João call it a shared discrepancy rather than a breach?
A Because the supplier had signed a confession.
B Because neither side had checked the portal and neither had flagged the amendment — it was a grey area both had fallen into, not an obligation one side had knowingly avoided.
C Because the amendment had never actually been approved.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Perfect & Past Perfect Continuous

GRAMMAR

The tense that reconstructs a sequence — and why the order of events decides the liability.

StructureWhat it doesIn the report
had + past participle
past perfect		The EARLIER of two past events — the past before the past. It fixes the ORDER, and in a dispute the order is the liability."By the time we flagged it, they had already shipped the units."
had been + verb -ing
past perfect continuous		HOW LONG something had been going on up to that past point. It shows the problem was not new, and it justifies the scale of it."They had been manufacturing to the old revision for three months."
by the time / before / when + PASTThese words set the LATER past moment; the earlier action then takes the past perfect. Both clauses are in the past — the perfect just says which came first."By the time the audit began, revision C had been superseded."
already / just / never / not yetalready = it had happened before then; never / not yet = it had not happened up to then. These words live inside the past perfect."We had never received the amendment, and they had already closed the file."
How it SOUNDSIn speech had almost never survives as a full word: it collapses to 'd. we had becomes "we'd", they had been becomes "they'd been", it had becomes "it'd". The tense is carried by the PARTICIPLE at the end (shipped, superseded, changed), not by the little 'd at the front. Stop hunting for a stressed had: listen for the participle, and you will hear the order.

Watch out (two classic mistakes): (1) "When we caught it, the supplier already shipped the units" — the earlier event needs had + past participle: had already shipped. Portuguese lets one simple past do the whole sequence, so the order disappears; (2) "They were manufacturing for three months before..." — a duration running up to a past point takes the past perfect continuous: had been manufacturing, never the plain past continuous.

Fine-print collocations: an amendment supersedes a revision (never "cancels") · a contract stipulates a requirement · a document takes precedence · to spell out a provision · to flag a discrepancy · to fix an annex reference.

Reconstructing a sequence (learn the frame by heart): "By the time X happened, Y had already happened." · "They had been doing Z for [time] before..." · "Neither side had flagged it, so..." — this is how a non-conformance stops being a fight and becomes a timeline.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — and notice how the "had" almost disappears into a 'd.

"By the time we caught the discrepancy, the supplier the forty units."
"They to revision C for three months before the amendment reached them."
"Before the units left the factory, the amendment revision C."
"Nobody the portal, so the discrepancy had gone unnoticed for weeks."
"The contract had the standard in its current revision."
"Which document takes , the contract or the standard?"

Stage 2: Put the Non-Conformance in Order

Order

Put the events of the dispute in the order they actually happened — from the purchase order to the shared agreement.

?Unknown to both sides, a committee amendment supersedes revision C and rewrites one provision in Annex D.
?They agree it is a shared discrepancy and fix the annex reference in the contract.
?The purchase order arrives: revision C is current, and the supplier certifies the units to it.
?At the factory acceptance test, the inspector opens Annex D and finds the discrepancy.
?Both sides reconstruct the timeline and see that neither had checked the portal.
?The supplier ships the first batch, still built to revision C, three months later.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Say it fast, running the words together: had collapses into a 'd (they'd already shipped) — producing the reduced form is what trains your ear to catch it. Watch the stress: soo-per-SEED, dis-KREP-an-cy, PRESS-e-dence.

By the time we caught the discrepancy, the supplier had already shipped the units.
They had been manufacturing to a superseded revision for three months.
The amendment had changed one provision in Annex D.
Which document takes precedence here, the contract or the standard?
Before we sign anything, I want that provision spelled out.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the dispute call — the reply that proves the order of events instead of assigning blame.

Sabine says the units were compliant when they left the factory. The strongest reply is:
A "When the units left the factory, revision C had already been superseded by the amendment, so they were built to a revision that no longer applied."
B "You are right, if they were compliant then, the case is closed."
C "Compliance does not matter once the units have shipped."
You want to explain why the inspector only caught the problem in April. You say:
A "The inspector was simply too slow to check."
B "By the time the inspector opened Annex D, the units had already been assembled and shipped, and nobody had cross-checked which revision was current."
C "There was no way anyone could have found the discrepancy."
Sabine argues the contract never named a specific revision. You push back:
A "You are correct, so there is no obligation at all."
B "The wording stipulated the standard in its current revision, and by the shipping date the current revision had already changed, so the requirement had moved with it."
C "The wording is not important; let us just move on."
She suggests accepting the units as they are, to save time. You answer:
A "Fine, if that is easier for your team, we will accept them."
B "I will consider it, with one caveat: I want that provision spelled out and the annex reference fixed before we sign anything."
C "That decision is not mine to make."
She asks whose fault the whole thing was. You reply:
A "It was entirely your fault, because you shipped the units."
B "Neither side had flagged the amendment and neither had updated its documents, so this is a shared discrepancy, not a breach."
C "It was entirely our fault, so we will absorb the cost."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. Tone: calm, precise, and always proving which event came first.

You are on the dispute call with the supplier's contracts manager. In two minutes: reconstruct the sequence of the non-conformance in the past perfect (what had already happened by the time your inspector flagged it, and how long each side had been working from the superseded revision), cite the wording of the contract, apply the order of precedence, concede your own side's missed update while holding theirs, and close by proposing a caveat: nothing is accepted until the provision is spelled out and the annex reference is fixed.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1By the time we caught the discrepancy, the supplier had already shipped the units.
2They had been manufacturing to a superseded revision for three months.
3Neither side had flagged it, so the discrepancy was shared, not a breach.
4Which document takes precedence here -- the contract, or the standard?
5Before we sign anything, I want that provision spelled out and the annex reference fixed.
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

Making Your Case -- Presenting Technical Findings

A three-month commissioning slip on Line 4 has to be presented to a European client's senior engineer. This is how you make the case: structure before detail -- an executive summary and a headline figure first, then findings, recommendation, and one clear call to action -- delivered under hard questions without freezing. Key words: executive summary, findings, recommendation, supporting data, headline figure, takeaway, to walk someone through, to highlight, to take questions, to circle back to, call to action, to wrap up. Structure: reduced relative clauses -- "the equipment (which was) delivered late", "the team (that is) running the test".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Last week you read the fine print of a contract. Tonight you stop reading other people's cases and make your own -- a structured technical update a busy client can act on in minutes. These are the words of a presentation that gets a decision: how you open, how you point to the numbers, how you take questions, and how you close with a clear ask. Listen to each term and read the example.

Executive summary -- a short opening section that states the key points for a busy decision-maker
"The executive summary is three sentences: what happened, the cause, and what I'm asking for."
Findings -- the results or conclusions your investigation has produced
"The findings point to a single component: one late axle-counter delivery."
Recommendation -- the course of action you advise, based on the findings
"My recommendation is a three-workstream recovery plan run on night shifts."
Supporting data -- the figures and evidence that back up a claim
"The supporting data attached to the note shows a realistic four-week recovery."
Headline figure -- the single most important number you want the audience to remember
"Twelve weeks lost, four to recover -- four weeks is the headline figure I lead with."
Takeaway -- the one main point you want people to leave the room with
"If they remember one thing, the takeaway is: recoverable to four weeks."
To walk someone through -- to guide someone step by step through something
"Let me walk you through the recovery plan, one workstream at a time."
To highlight -- to draw attention to the most important point
"I'll highlight one figure on this slide and let the handout carry the rest."
To take questions -- to invite and answer questions from the audience
"I'll take any questions now -- and if you can approve the access, we hold the date."
To circle back to -- to return to a topic mentioned earlier
"That question comes a little early -- can I circle back to the cost in two slides?"
Call to action -- a clear statement of what you want the audience to do next
"The call to action is one decision: approve night-shift access on this call."
To wrap up -- to bring a point or a presentation to a close
"To wrap up: one slip, one cause, one plan, and one decision I need from you."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Executive summary
Findings
Recommendation
Supporting data
Headline figure
Takeaway
To walk someone through
To highlight
To take questions
To circle back to
Call to action
To wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

The interlocking commissioning on Line 4 has slipped by three months, and this afternoon João Guilherme presents it to Nordic Rail's senior engineer. His manager's brief is exact: do not open with the apology or the detail. Open with the executive summary -- one sentence on what happened, one on the cause, one on the ask -- and lead with the headline figure: a twelve-week slip, recoverable to four. A busy client decides in the first minute whether to trust you.

The grammar of a technical briefing is dense, and one structure does most of the work: the reduced relative clause. Watch how a full relative clause loses its who / which / that + be. "The equipment which was delivered late" becomes "the equipment delivered late" -- a past participle, because the equipment receives the action. "The team that is running the test" becomes "the team running the test" -- an -ing form, because the team does the action. So the findings read like this: "the axle counters manufactured in Shenzhen arrived late, and two of the units delivered failed the inspection carried out at our depot."

Then the recommendation, and only then the ask. João will walk the client through the recovery plan, highlight the one number in the supporting data, and close with a single call to action: approve the night-shift access. If a hard question interrupts him and the answer is not ready, he will not freeze or invent -- he will say, "that's a good question, let me think about that for a moment", give the part he is sure of, and promise to circle back to the detail. The takeaway he wants them to leave with is one line: recoverable to four weeks. Then he will take questions and wrap up.

1. "The equipment delivered late was the cause of the slip." Why is it delivered and not delivering?
A Because -ing and past participle mean the same thing here.
B Because the equipment RECEIVES the action (it was delivered) -- passive, so the past participle. The full clause was "which was delivered".
C Because delivered is a noun here.
2. Which reduced relative clause is correct?
A "The team ran the test flagged the issue."
B "The team running the test flagged the issue."
C "The team who running the test flagged the issue."
3. The manager tells João to open with the executive summary and the headline figure. Why?
A Because the client wants a long, detailed story first.
B Because a busy client decides in the first minute whether to trust you -- structure before detail.
C Because the summary replaces the recommendation.
4. A hard question interrupts and João's answer is not ready. What does he do?
A Invents a number so he doesn't look unprepared.
B Names the silence -- "let me think about that for a moment" -- gives the part he is sure of, and promises to circle back to the detail.
C Stops the presentation until he finds the answer.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reduced Relative Clauses

GRAMMAR

A relative clause loses its who / which / that + be. What is left is a participle -- and one question decides which one: does the noun DO the action, or RECEIVE it?

Full relative clauseReduced formWhy
the equipment which was delivered latethe equipment delivered lateThe noun RECEIVES the action → past participle (passive).
the team that is running the testthe team running the testThe noun DOES the action → -ing form (active).
the units that are still in transitthe units still in transitA state or a phrase → drop that + be, keep the description.
the supplier that we choseno reduction → use "chosen by us"Here that is the OBJECT. You can only reduce a subject relative clause.
Why it mattersReduced relative clauses are what make a briefing note read as dense and precise: "the recommendation set out below", "the action requested from the client", "the data attached to this note". Half the noun phrases in a real technical document are reduced relatives -- and producing them is what makes your English sound professional, not just correct.

Watch out (habits carried over from Portuguese): (1) "The data presenting on this slide..." -- the data RECEIVES the action, so use the PAST PARTICIPLE: "the data presented on this slide". Ask: does it present, or is it presented? (2) "The supplier chosen for Line 4, it missed the deadline" -- once you reduce the clause, do NOT repeat the subject: "the supplier chosen for Line 4 missed the deadline"; (3) "the engineer who presenting..." -- drop who/which/that AND be together: "the engineer presenting...".

Presentation collocations: present findings, outline a recommendation, support a claim with data, walk someone through a plan, draw attention to a figure, highlight a key finding, take questions, circle back to a point, wrap up with a call to action.

The question to ask before you write a technical noun phrase: "Does this noun DO the action or RECEIVE it?" Does it → -ing. Receives it → past participle. That one test gives you the whole grammar.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct participle or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The equipment late was the single cause of the slip."
"The team the factory test flagged the firmware issue."
"I'll open with a three-sentence ."
"Let me you through the recovery plan, one workstream at a time."
"That's a good question -- let me think about that for a moment; I'll the exact date after the call."
"To wrap up, the one is a decision: approve night-shift access."

Stage 2: Build the Presentation in Order

Order

Put the moves of a structured technical update in order -- from the executive summary to taking questions.

?Close with one call to action: approve the night-shift access to hold the opening date.
?Present the findings: the axle counters delivered late were the single cause.
?Open with the executive summary and the headline figure: twelve weeks, recoverable to four.
?Take questions -- and if a hard one lands, name the silence and promise to circle back.
?Outline the recommendation: a three-workstream recovery run on night shifts.
?Draw attention to the one number in the supporting data, and let the handout carry the rest.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Stress "eg-ZEK-yu-tiv" and "rek-uh-men-DAY-shun" on the right syllable, and keep the reduced participles crisp: "delivered", "set out", "running".

Let me start with the headline figure: a twelve-week slip, recoverable to four.
The equipment delivered late was the single cause of the slip.
I'll draw your attention to one figure and let the handout carry the rest.
That's a good question -- let me think about that for a moment, and I'll circle back to the detail.
To wrap up: one slip, one cause, one plan, and one decision I need from you today.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the best answer for every real moment of the presentation -- the opening, the data, the hard question, the timing, and the close.

You open the status update to a busy senior engineer. Your strongest first move is:
A "Let me explain everything from the beginning -- in January we ran the factory test, then..."
B "The headline is a twelve-week slip, recoverable to four; one late component caused it, and I need one decision today."
C "I'm so sorry, this is a serious delay and it's been very difficult on our side."
The slide shows a table with twelve numbers. To highlight the key finding, you:
A Read every row of the table out loud in order.
B Draw attention to the one figure that matters and let the handout carry the rest.
C Skip the data entirely and hope no one asks.
A hard question interrupts you and you don't have the exact answer ready. You say:
A Anything, quickly, to fill the silence -- even a number you're not sure of.
B "That's a good question -- let me think about that for a moment," give the part you're sure of, and promise to circle back.
C Nothing -- you go silent and lose the thread.
A question belongs later in your presentation. Without losing your thread, you say:
A "That's exactly where I'm heading -- can I circle back to the cost in two slides, once the plan is on the table?"
B "I don't know, let's talk about it now instead."
C Ignore the question and keep going as if it wasn't asked.
You reach the end of the update. To close with a clean call to action, you say:
A "To wrap up: one slip, one cause, one plan -- and one decision I need from you today, which is night-shift access."
B "So, that's more or less everything, I think. Any thoughts?"
C "There are about ten things I'd like you to approve, in no particular order."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and with no stopping to correct yourself. Structure: summary, findings, recommendation, call to action.

Deliver the opening two minutes of your Line 4 status update to Nordic Rail. Lead with the executive summary and the headline figure, present the findings using at least one reduced relative clause ("the equipment delivered late", "the team running the test"), highlight one number, and close with a single call to action. If you imagine a hard question, name the silence and circle back -- do not freeze.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Let me start with the headline figure, then walk you through the findings and the recommendation.
2The equipment delivered late was the single cause of the slip.
3I'll draw your attention to one figure and let the handout carry the rest.
4That's a good question -- let me think about that for a moment, and I'll circle back to the detail.
5To wrap up: one slip, one cause, one plan -- and one decision I need from you today.
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Speaking Under Pressure -- Handling Tough Questions in Real Time

The Belgian interview, run again -- but this time with the tools to stay in control when a question lands on the spot. How to buy yourself time out loud, stall professionally, reframe a hostile question as information, and keep your cool instead of going silent. Key words: under pressure, on the spot, to improvise, to stall for time, to pivot, to reframe, to deflect, to manage expectations, to save face, to buy yourself some time, to turn the tables, to keep your cool, to read the room, to maintain composure. Structure: the modals that let you advise, prefer and distance without sounding weak -- I'd rather address this directly, you had better confirm that in writing, we may as well reschedule the FAT.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Last week you presented findings and took questions from a friendly client. This week the questions come at you hostile, in an interview, with no slides to hide behind. These are the words of staying in control when a question lands on the spot. Listen to each term and read the example.

Under pressure -- in a stressful situation that demands you perform right now
"He answers well under pressure, even when the question is hostile."
On the spot -- forced to answer or act immediately, with no time to prepare
"She put me on the spot with a question I had not expected."
To improvise -- to respond without preparation, using whatever you have in the moment
"When the slide failed, I had to improvise the whole explanation."
To stall for time -- to delay on purpose so you have a moment to think before you answer
"Repeating the question is a professional way to stall for time."
To pivot -- to change your approach smoothly when the first one stops working
"When she pushed back, I pivoted from the numbers to the recovery plan."
To reframe -- to present a problem in a different, more useful light
"I reframed the delay as a problem I had diagnosed and controlled."
To deflect -- to turn a hostile question aside without answering it head-on
"He deflected the personal jab and brought it back to the project."
To manage expectations -- to shape what someone believes will happen, so it stays realistic
"I'd rather manage their expectations early than apologize later."
To save face -- to protect your dignity or reputation in an awkward moment
"Owning the mistake let me save face better than hiding it would have."
To buy yourself some time -- to create a short, professional pause so you can gather your thoughts
"Let me pull up the data -- a clean way to buy yourself some time."
To turn the tables -- to reverse a disadvantage so the situation now works in your favor
"One good clarifying question turned the tables and put her on the back foot."
To keep your cool -- to stay calm and in control when the pressure rises
"She interrupted me twice, but I kept my cool and stayed on message."
To read the room -- to sense the mood of the people in front of you and adjust what you say
"He read the room, saw they were skeptical, and slowed down."
To maintain composure -- to stay outwardly calm and professional under stress
"Maintaining your composure matters more than having a perfect answer."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Under pressure
On the spot
To improvise
To stall for time
To pivot
To reframe
To deflect
To manage expectations
To save face
To buy yourself some time
To turn the tables
To keep your cool
To read the room
To maintain composure

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last March, an interview with a Belgian rail company ended in silence. João knew his field, but when the questions stopped following his script, his answers stopped coming, and he walked out feeling he had failed. Tomorrow he faces the same company again — and this time the goal is not a perfect answer, it is control. Élise Laurent, the technical director, has been clear: she will interrupt him, she will ask why a project slipped three months under his watch, and she will not soften it. She is not testing his engineering. She is testing what he does when a question lands on the spot.

The reflex he is building is simple. When the hostile question comes, he does not improvise something he does not believe. He buys himself some time out loud: "That's a fair question — could you clarify what you mean by 'under my watch'?" The clarifying question stalls professionally, moves the pressure back across the table, and makes him sound senior. Then he reframes the delay: not a failure that controlled him, but a problem he diagnosed early and kept under control. If she jabs at his credentials, he deflects the jab and brings it back to the project. Through all of it, he keeps his cool and reads the room, because the point was never to hide the delay — it was to talk about one without falling apart.

His grammar carries the tone. He would rather address the delay directly than talk around it. He tells a wavering supplier that they had better confirm the date in writing before he commits. When the timeline will not move, he says they may as well plan the recovery around it now. These small modals are not decoration — would rather chooses, had better advises, may as well accepts reality without apology, and together they are the sound of a person thinking clearly while the room pushes. By the end of the call, the delay he had been so afraid of has become the best answer he gives all day.

1. "I'd rather address the delay directly than talk around it." What does would rather signal here?
A That João is unsure and wants to avoid the topic.
B A calm PREFERENCE between two options — he chooses the direct path over the evasive one, which signals control, not avoidance.
C That he is giving the interviewer a strict order.
2. "You had better confirm the date in writing before we commit." Why had better and not just should?
A Because had better is only correct in the past tense.
B Because had better is strong advice with an implied consequence — do it, or something bad follows — given from authority, not from fear.
C Because should is grammatically wrong before a verb.
3. When Élise puts him on the spot, why does João repeat a clarifying question instead of answering at once?
A Because he did not understand any of the question.
B Because it stalls professionally: it buys thinking time, moves the pressure back across the table, and sounds like rigor rather than delay.
C Because interviewers require every answer to start with a question.
4. "The timeline is fixed, so we may as well plan the recovery around it." What does may as well mean?
A That planning the recovery is a bad idea he is forced into.
B That, given the situation, this is the sensible move left — he accepts reality without apologizing for it.
C That there are still many better options he is ignoring.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Would Rather / Had Better / May as Well

GRAMMAR

Three modals that let you advise, prefer and accept reality without ever sounding cornered.

StructureWhat it doesIn the room
would rather + bare verb ... than ...
preference		You choose one option over another, calmly and on the record. It signals CONTROL, not avoidance. The verb after it takes NO to."I'd rather address the delay directly than talk around it."
had better + bare verb
strong advice / warning		Near an order, but softer — there is an implied "or something bad happens". You give it from authority. It is HAD better, never would better."You had better confirm that date in writing before we commit."
may / might as well + bare verb
the best option left		Given the situation, this is the sensible move — you accept reality without apologizing for it. A calm, senior kind of shrug."If the equipment hasn't arrived, we may as well reschedule the FAT now."
Bare infinitive — all threeNone of them takes to: never "would rather TO address", never "had better TO confirm". The verb stays bare."We'd better lock the scope now." / "I'd rather wait than rush it."
How it SOUNDSIn speech would and had both collapse to 'd: I would rather becomes "I'd rather", you had better becomes "you'd better". Do not confuse this 'd with the past perfect's 'd (they'd shipped): here the 'd is followed by a bare infinitive (rather address, better confirm), not by a past participle. Listen for what comes after the 'd, and you will always know which one it is.

Watch out (three classic mistakes): (1) "I would rather TO address it" — drop the to: would rather address; (2) "You WOULD better confirm" — it is HAD better, not would better; (3) "We may as well TO reschedule" — bare verb again: may as well reschedule. All three take a plain infinitive with no to.

Pressure collocations: to buy yourself some time · to keep your cool · to read the room · to manage expectations · to stall for time · to turn the tables · to save face · to be put on the spot.

The reflex to learn by heart: hostile question → "That's a fair question — could you clarify...?" (buy time) → reframe the problem as one you controlled → answer with a modal that keeps you senior (I'd rather... / you had better... / we may as well...). A pause you own reads as confidence; a pause you hide reads as panic.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence -- and notice how the "would" and "had" both collapse into a 'd, with a plain verb right after.

"I address the delay directly than let it define the interview."
"You confirm the delivery date in writing before you commit to it."
"The equipment is already late, so we reschedule the test now."
"Let me pull up the data -- a clean way to yourself some time without going silent."
"Instead of treating the question as an attack, it as a request for information."
"She interrupts you twice; the skill is to your cool and stay on message."

Stage 2: Put the Pressure Moment in Order

Order

Put the steps of handling a hostile question in the order a calm professional actually takes them -- from the question landing to finishing back in control.

?The interviewer lands a hostile question about the three-month delay, with no warning.
?You reframe the delay as a problem you diagnosed early and kept under control.
?You stall professionally with a clarifying question, moving the pressure back across the table.
?You read the room, invite the next question, and finish back in control of the conversation.
?You keep your cool and buy yourself a moment instead of rushing to fill the silence.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Say it at real speed: would and had collapse into a 'd (I'd rather, you'd better) -- and a plain verb follows, never a "to". A pause you own reads as confidence.

That's a fair question -- let me take a moment before I answer it.
I'd rather give you the right number than a quick one.
You had better confirm that date in writing before we commit.
If the timeline is fixed, we may as well plan the recovery around it.
Let me reframe that: the delay was diagnosed early and kept under control.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each pressure moment of the interview -- the reply that keeps you in control instead of caving or improvising.

Élise asks, sharply, why the project slipped three months under your watch. The strongest reply is:
A "That's a fair question -- could you clarify whether you mean the root cause or how I managed it once it appeared?"
B "It was not really my fault, so I would rather not discuss it."
C "Um, well, these things happen on big projects."
She interrupts and challenges a figure you just gave. You want to stay in control. You say:
A "You are right, I am probably wrong about that number."
B "Let me pull up the exact figure -- I'd rather confirm it than guess on the record."
C "I do not remember the number, let us move on."
A supplier on a live call will not commit to a delivery date. You give firm advice:
A "You had better confirm that date in writing today, because the FAT schedule depends on it."
B "You would better send me the date whenever you can."
C "It is fine, take all the time you need."
The equipment clearly will not arrive in time and the meeting keeps arguing about it. You say:
A "We should keep waiting and hope it arrives."
B "The date isn't going to hold, so we may as well reschedule the FAT now and tell the client today."
C "There is nothing anyone can do about this."
Élise says the delay makes her doubt you can handle a bigger project. You reframe and turn the tables:
A "Maybe you are right that I am not ready for it."
B "I understand the concern; what it actually shows is how I behave when a project goes wrong -- I diagnosed it early and kept it under control."
C "That is unfair, the delay was not my responsibility at all."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for two minutes, with no script and without stopping to correct yourself. Tone: calm, honest, always in control -- reframe the delay instead of apologizing for it.

You are in the interview and Élise asks, sharply: "Why did this project slip three months under your watch, and why should I trust you with a bigger one?" In two minutes: buy yourself a moment out loud, ask a clarifying question if you need one, reframe the delay as a problem you diagnosed early and kept under control, concede honestly what you would do differently, and use at least one of would rather, had better or may as well naturally. Do not fill the silence with noise -- own it.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1That's a fair question -- give me a moment to think it through.
2I'd rather address the delay directly than talk around it.
3Let me make sure I understand -- are you asking about the cause or the recovery?
4If the timeline is fixed, we may as well plan the recovery around it now.
5You had better lock that delivery date in writing before we commit.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

Holding Your Ground -- Defending Technical Decisions Under Pressure

A Spanish signaling supplier says the four-week overrun on Line 4 is Motiva's fault and files an extension-of-time claim you do not accept. This is how you hold your ground without conceding: acknowledge the concern once, dispute the claim with a dated record, and close with a next step. Key words: schedule overrun, extension of time, burden of proof, to substantiate, to dispute, unfounded, to attribute, to reserve your rights, without prejudice, to rebut, back-charge, concurrent delay. Structure: the mandative subjunctive -- "It is essential that the supplier submit the plan", "We recommend that the team be notified".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Last week you made your case in a presentation. Tonight the case comes back at you: a supplier claims the delay was your fault and files a claim. These are the words of a formal dispute -- how you name a delay claim, how you challenge it, and how you protect your position without closing the door. Listen to each term and read the example.

Schedule overrun -- the amount of time a project runs past its agreed completion date
"The schedule overrun is four weeks, and the baseline shows the cause was not on our side."
Extension of time -- extra contract time a supplier formally requests to cover a delay
"The supplier has filed an extension-of-time claim we do not accept."
Burden of proof -- the duty to prove that a claim is true
"The burden of proof for an extension of time rests with the party requesting it."
To substantiate -- to support a claim with documented evidence
"A claim you cannot substantiate from the sign-off log will not stand."
To dispute -- to formally challenge that a claim or charge is correct
"We dispute the claim that the delay was caused by our documentation."
Unfounded -- not supported by facts or evidence
"The claim that we approved the drawings late is unfounded on the record."
To attribute -- to state that something was caused by a particular party or thing
"The supplier attributes the delay to us; the timeline attributes it to the late units."
To reserve your rights -- to keep the option of taking formal action later
"We reserve our rights under the penalty clause while we resolve this in good faith."
Without prejudice -- said or written so it cannot be used against you later in a dispute
"Without prejudice, I can offer to share the cost of the retest while we review the log."
To rebut -- to give evidence or arguments that show a claim is wrong
"Let me rebut that with the inspection log carried out on the nineteenth."
Back-charge -- a cost the buyer deducts from a supplier for work the buyer had to correct
"If the units fail again, Motiva will raise a back-charge for the rework."
Concurrent delay -- a delay for which both parties are partly responsible at the same time
"There is no concurrent delay here; the record points to one side only."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each term with the correct definition.

Schedule overrun
Extension of time
Burden of proof
To substantiate
To dispute
Unfounded
To attribute
To reserve your rights
Without prejudice
To rebut
Back-charge
Concurrent delay

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions below.

Ibersign, a Spanish signaling supplier, has filed an extension of time for a four-week schedule overrun on Line 4, and it attributes the delay to Motiva's late approval of the interface drawings. João Guilherme's job this afternoon is not to win the argument and not to give in. It is to hold his ground: acknowledge the concern once, then dispute the claim with the dated record.

The grammar that makes a position sound like a requirement instead of a plea is the mandative subjunctive. After words of urgency or requirement, the verb drops its ending and stays in the base form for every subject. "It is essential that the supplier submit the revised plan" -- submit, not submits. "We recommend that the team be notified in writing" -- be, not is. The negative is simply not + base: "We request that the units not be shipped until the fix is confirmed" -- no do or does. The base form is what turns an opinion ("the supplier needs to submit") into a stated term.

So João holds the line. The burden of proof for the extension rests with Ibersign, and the claim is unfounded on the sign-off log: the drawings were approved on the fourth, and the units arrived six weeks later, so there is no concurrent delay on Motiva's side. He can rebut the claim, substantiate every date, and still keep the relationship: without prejudice, he recommends both sides review the log before anyone talks about a back-charge, and he reserves Motiva's rights under the penalty clause. Firm on the facts, warm on the person, the door still open.

1. "It is essential that the supplier submit the plan." Why submit and not submits?
A Because it is a spelling mistake — it should be submits.
B Because after "it is essential that" the verb stays in the BASE form for every subject — the mandative subjunctive. It states a requirement, not an opinion.
C Because submit is a noun here.
2. Which sentence is correct?
A "We recommend that the team is notified in writing."
B "We recommend that the team be notified in writing."
C "We recommend that the team being notified in writing."
3. According to the text, who carries the burden of proof for the extension of time?
A Motiva, because it approved the drawings.
B Ibersign — the party requesting the extension. The claim is unfounded on the dated sign-off log.
C Neither side — it is a concurrent delay.
4. How does João dispute the claim and still keep the relationship?
A He concedes the point to avoid a conflict.
B He rebuts the claim with the record and reserves Motiva's rights, but — without prejudice — proposes both sides review the log before any back-charge.
C He raises his voice and blames the supplier's equipment.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Mandative Subjunctive

GRAMMAR

After certain words of requirement, the verb loses its ending and its do/does. One question decides the form: is this a stated requirement, or just an opinion? If it is a requirement, use the base form.

TriggerSubjunctive formWhy
it is essential / critical / important that…the supplier submit the planBase form for every subject → no -s on he/she/it. It states a requirement.
require / recommend / insist / request / propose that…the team be notifiedThe subjunctive of be is simply be for all persons → not is / are.
the negativethe units not be shippedJust not + base → no do/does. "not ship", "not be filed".
opinion vs requirement"the supplier submits" → opinion"the supplier submit" → a stated term. Same words, different force.
Why it mattersIn a dispute, the subjunctive lets you be firm and impersonal at once. "It is critical that the schedule remain unchanged" is not an attack and not a plea — it is a condition you are setting. It is the grammar of a person who sets the terms, not one who reacts to them.

Watch out (habits carried over from Portuguese): (1) "It is essential that the supplier submits…" — after "essential that", drop the -s: "the supplier submit". (2) "We recommend that the team is notified" — use be: "the team be notified". (3) "We request that the units do not ship" — the negative is not + base: "the units not be shipped".

Diplomatic hedging (firm but not aggressive): "To some extent I understand the pressure, however…", "One could argue that the baseline was already agreed", "With respect, the record doesn't support that", "I hear the concern, but I can't concede a point the record doesn't support."

The question to ask before you write a formal clause: "Is this a requirement, or my opinion?" A requirement → base form (submit, be, remain). That one test gives you the whole grammar.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct base-form verb or phrase. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"It is essential that the supplier the revised test plan."
"We recommend that the team notified in writing."
"With respect, the record doesn't support that; the timeline the overrun to the late delivery, not to us."
"The for the extension rests with the party requesting it."
", I recommend that both sides review the sign-off log first."
"On the record, the claim that we approved the drawings late is ."

Stage 2: Hold Your Ground in Order

Order

Put the moves of holding your ground in order -- from acknowledging the concern to closing with a next step.

?State the requirement in the subjunctive: it is essential that any claim be substantiated from the log first.
?Acknowledge the concern once, genuinely: "I understand, and I've taken it seriously."
?Close with a next step, not a verdict: without prejudice, review the log together and share the retest cost.
?Dispute the claim with the dated record: the drawings were approved on the fourth, countersigned.
?Hold the line on the burden of proof: I can't concede a point the record doesn't support.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. Stress "sub-STAN-shee-ate" and "PREJ-uh-dis" on the right syllable, and keep the base-form verbs crisp: "submit", "be notified", "remain".

I understand the concern, and I've taken it seriously — but let me put the record on the table.
With respect, the record doesn't support that — the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery.
It is essential that any claim be substantiated from the sign-off log first.
The burden of proof rests with the party requesting the extension, and I can't concede what the record doesn't support.
Without prejudice, I recommend that both sides review the log before anyone talks about a back-charge.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Pick the best answer for every real moment of the claim meeting -- the accusation, the requirement, the pressure on cost, and the close.

The supplier says: "The delay is on your side." Your strongest first move is:
A "Well, maybe we were a little late, I'm not sure…"
B "I understand the concern, and I've taken it seriously — but the dated sign-off log doesn't support it."
C "That's absurd — it's your equipment that failed."
You need to state a formal requirement about the revised plan. You say:
A "It is essential that the supplier submits the plan."
B "It is essential that the supplier submit a revised plan that accounts for the two failed units."
C "It is essential that the supplier is submitting the plan."
The supplier pushes: "Someone has to pay for this retest." You hold the line by saying:
A "To some extent I understand the pressure — but the burden of proof rests with the party requesting the extension, and I can't concede what the record doesn't support."
B "Fine, we'll cover it — I don't want a conflict."
C "That's your problem, not ours."
The claim is unfounded on the log. To rebut it without heat, you say:
A "You're wrong and you know it."
B "Let me rebut that with the record: the drawings were approved on the fourth, so the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery."
C "I'd rather not get into the details."
You want to protect Motiva without closing the door. You say:
A "We reserve our rights under the penalty clause, but — without prejudice — I recommend we review the log together and share the retest cost pending that review."
B "This is final. There's nothing more to discuss."
C "Let's just forget the whole thing."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. Speak for 2 minutes, with no script and with no stopping to correct yourself. Structure: acknowledge, hold on the facts, state a requirement, close with a next step.

The supplier has just told you the Line 4 delay is Motiva's fault and filed for an extension of time. Respond as João. Acknowledge the concern once without conceding, dispute the claim using the dated sign-off log, state at least one requirement in the subjunctive ("It is essential that… submit / be"), hold the line on the burden of proof, and close — without prejudice — with a next step that keeps the relationship. Stay calm; do not apologize and do not give in.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1I understand the concern, and I've taken it seriously — but let me put the record on the table.
2With respect, the record doesn't support that — the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery.
3The burden of proof rests with the party requesting the extension — I can't concede what the record doesn't support.
4It is essential that any claim be substantiated from the sign-off log first.
5Without prejudice, I recommend that both sides review the log before anyone talks about a back-charge.

Complementary Materials

Extra input to keep the language alive between classes. Check the box once you have watched or listened to it.

Lesson 1 — Diagnostic + Reactivation -- Who Is João Guilherme in English?

Series
Brooklyn Nine-Nine -- Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

Your own show — this time with a target. In the pilot, a new captain walks in and every single character has to say who they are, what they do and how long they have been doing it. Same task as this lesson, at native speed: exactly the pace you said you struggle to follow.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments in which someone describes their job or their track record — and label the tense (present simple? present perfect? past simple?).

Watch ↗
Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) -- any episode

Every episode is one person telling their own career story: what they DO today (present simple), how long they have been doing it (present perfect, for/since) and what they DID in one specific year (past simple). It is the grammar of this lesson in the wild, in the business register you need.

Tip: listen to 15 minutes ONE time, no pausing. Then retell the opening of the story out loud in 60 seconds — record it and play it back. Your bottleneck is production, not comprehension.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Learn English Tenses: The Present Perfect Continuous

The structure you will lean on in every status meeting, and the one you have never produced with confidence: have been + verb-ing for whatever started and is STILL going on ("We've been waiting for the specification since March", "I've been working with German suppliers since 2019"). The lesson introduces it; this video digs deeper.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR project at Motiva — two in the present perfect continuous (something still going on), two in the present perfect (for/since) and one in the past simple (finished time). Out loud, with nothing written down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Reactivating Your Professional Voice -- Roles, Responsibilities and Ongoing Projects

Series
Formula 1: Drive to Survive — any season (Netflix)

Engineers and team principals doing, episode after episode, exactly what you practiced today: explaining what went wrong in a race without destroying the supplier, the driver or the team. The third conditional shows up in the wild — "if we had pitted one lap earlier, we would have won the race".

Tip: watch with English subtitles and write down 3 sentences shaped like "if we had..., we would have...". Then rewrite each one, swapping the race for YOUR project at Motiva.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — "Why Project-Based Work Fails — and How to Get It Right"

A full episode on why projects run late: scope, suppliers, interfaces and the hard conversation nobody wants to have. It is the vocabulary of this lesson (workstream, procurement, handover, lead time) in the business English your next international interview will use.

Tip: listen to 15 minutes ONCE, without pausing. Then record 60 seconds summarizing the main argument out loud — the bottleneck is your speaking, not your ear.

Listen ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — How to use the third conditional (6 Minute Grammar)

Six minutes, a clean British accent, and the exact structure of this lesson: if + had + past participle, would have + past participle. Pay close attention to the contractions in speech ("if they'd sent it, we'd have caught the error") — that is how the sentence reaches your ear on a call, never spelled out.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR project — three in the third conditional (one of them negative) and two describing your role with "I'm responsible for" and "My role involves". Out loud, with nothing written down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Running and Following Status Meetings -- Reporting, Updating and Pushing Back

Documentary
Downfall: The Case Against Boeing (Netflix, 1h29)

A whole documentary about what happens when a non-conformance is not flagged up, when a waiver is granted to save factory time, and when nobody triggers the escalation in time. This is your technical world — certification, inspection, supplier and regulator — in native English. And the modal perfects show up in the wild: "this should have been flagged", "they must have known".

Tip: watch it with English subtitles and write down 5 sentences with should have / must have / might have. Then rewrite each one, swapping the aircraft for YOUR signaling package at Motiva.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — "To Negotiate Better, Start with Yourself" (27 min)

William Ury, co-author of Getting to Yes and co-founder of the Harvard Program on Negotiation. His thesis is exactly what you practiced today: the biggest obstacle in a negotiation is almost never the other side — it is your own reaction when they hand the blame back to you. Listen to it with Mei in mind: how to push back without breaking the relationship.

Tip: listen to all 27 minutes ONCE, without pausing — native pace, no subtitles. Then record 60 seconds, out loud, answering: "What do I usually do when a supplier blames me for their own delay?" The bottleneck is your speaking, not your listening.

Listen ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — Modals in the past (BBC English Class)

The exact structure of the lesson, straight to the point: should have + past participle (the obligation that failed), must have + past participle (the deduction) and might have + past participle (the possibility). Pay close attention to the contractions in speech ("it should've been sent", "they must've missed it") — that is how the sentence reaches your ear on a call, never spelled out.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR project — two with should have (an overdue item on the punch list), two with must have (the cause you deduce) and one with might have (the hypothesis you have not confirmed yet). Out loud, with nothing written down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Navigating Conflict and Escalation -- Diplomatic Assertiveness in High-Stakes Negotiations

Movie
Dark Waters (Netflix, 2h06)

A corporate lawyer finds out that his own client is hiding environmental damage — and spends twenty years arguing liability, indemnity and breach of contract with the people who used to pay him. It is the vocabulary of this lesson in native English, spoken by professionals who live on it: clauses, documented evidence, escalation, and the moment you decide to bite the bullet and go all the way. Notice that nobody shouts: the power sits in the fact, in the document and in the formal record.

Tip: watch it with English subtitles and write down 5 lines where someone states a hard position in polite language. Then rewrite each one for YOUR signaling package at Motiva — swapping the chemical plant for the late schedule.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — "The Inherent Failures of Long-Term Contracts — and How to Fix Them" (25 min)

Exactly your problem, discussed by two contract researchers: why a long client-supplier contract ALWAYS reaches a point where the letter of the contract and the commercial relationship pull in opposite directions — and what to do when the penalty clause is there, but using it costs you the supplier. Listen to it with Katrin in mind: when to invoke, when to propose mediation, and why arbitration almost always means both sides have lost.

Tip: listen ONCE without pausing, at native speed, with no transcript. Then record 60 seconds answering out loud: "In my package, what would it actually cost us to invoke the penalty clause?" The bottleneck is your speaking, not your ear.

Listen ↗
YouTube
BBC English Masterclass — Inversion 1: After Negative or Limiting Adverbs

The exact structure of this lesson, straight to the point: what happens to the subject and the auxiliary after never, not only, hardly and under no circumstances. Pay close attention to the INTONATION: the stress falls on the trigger ("NEVER has...", "NOT ONLY did..."), and that is what gives the sentence its weight on a call.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR project — two with Never has... (a bad record on the package), two with Not only did... but also... (two supplier failures in a single sentence) and one with Under no circumstances will we... (the line you will not cross). Out loud, and without writing them down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Performing Under Pressure -- The International Job Interview

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1 "Pilot" (Netflix, 1h07)

The first 20 minutes are the best interview lesson ever filmed. Mike Ross walks into a room with no track record, no diploma and nothing to lose — and walks out hired, because he builds a value proposition in three sentences. Notice how often the lawyers use cleft sentences to push the decisive information to the end of the sentence: "What I do is...", "What you need is...". This is the English of somebody who owns the room.

Tip: watch it with English subtitles and write down 5 of Harvey's lines that start with What... or It was.... Then rewrite each one, swapping the law firm for YOUR signaling package at Motiva.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart (Stanford GSB) — #151 "Get Hired: How the Right Communication Can Advance Your Career" (26 min)

Matt Abrahams, from Stanford, with Andrew Seaman, careers editor at LinkedIn. The thesis is exactly the one behind this lesson: nobody hires your experience, they hire the NARRATIVE you build with it — and connecting the dots is your job, never the interviewer's. There is a section on your LinkedIn profile being "your first interview", which is your Task 2 this week.

Tip: listen to all 26 minutes ONCE, without pausing — native pace, no subtitles. Then record 60 seconds answering, out loud: "What I bring to my next role is..." If the sentence does not come out whole on the first try, do it again until it does. The bottleneck is the speaking, not the listening.

Listen ↗
YouTube
CLEFT SENTENCES — advanced complex sentences in English + TEST!

The exact structure of this lesson, with a test at the end: it-cleft ("It was in San Francisco that...") and wh-cleft ("What I bring to this role is..."). Pay special attention to the point that catches most speakers out: after What... the verb is always is, never are — and the it-cleft takes that, never where.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR career — two with What I... (what you deliver), two with It was... that (San Francisco, 2023; the interview in March) and one with The reason I... is that (why signaling). Out loud, with nothing written down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Holding Your Ground -- The Supplier Approval Meeting

Documentary
Downfall: The Case Against Boeing (Netflix, 1h29)

This is your lesson, on the scale of a tragedy. Engineers raise concerns about the MCAS; the company downplays the failure ("it is a minor change, no training required"); responsibility is pushed onto the pilot and onto the supplier (passing the buck); and the root cause is admitted only after 346 deaths. Listen to how the investigators speak: almost everything they assert is in the passive voice — "the sensor was found to be defective", "the certification had not been completed" — because an impersonal finding is the only kind that survives a courtroom.

Tip: watch it with English subtitles and write down 8 passive sentences said by investigators or executives. Then classify each one: who disappeared from the sentence, and did the sentence get stronger or more cowardly because of it? That is the difference between hiding behind the passive and stating a fact with it.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
Causality (John Chidgey) — Episode 62 "OceanGate Titan" (39 min)

An Australian electrical engineer does, episode after episode, exactly what you do in a factory acceptance test: root cause analysis. In this one, specialists raised concerns about the hull and the materials, the company refused third-party certification (the sign-off it did not want to ask for), and the submersible imploded. The English is technical and native, at real speed — and the structure of the reasoning (symptom → cause → corrective action) is the structure of your own non-conformance report.

Tip: listen to all 39 minutes ONCE, without pausing. Then record 60 seconds summarizing out loud what was found, what had not been done and what should have been implemented. If the sentence does not come out whole on the first try, do it again until it does.

Listen ↗
YouTube
HAVE / GET something DONE (CAUSATIVES) — English Grammar for B2 First (SMASH English)

The exact structure of this lesson, at the exact level: have something done and get something done. Pay attention to the point that trips up most engineers: the OBJECT comes before the participle — "have the report resubmitted", never "have resubmitted the report". And note the difference in meaning: I resubmitted it (I did the work) versus I had it resubmitted (I demanded it; somebody else did the work).

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR own signaling package — three with have something done (the report, the relay, the certificate) and two with the passive of a finding (was found to be..., had not been issued). Out loud, with nothing written down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Never Back Down -- The Quarterly Project Review

Documentary
Challenger: The Final Flight (Netflix, 4 episodes · watch EP. 3 and 4)

This is your lesson pushed to the limit: schedule pressure against an engineer who takes a stance. On the teleconference the night before, the Thiokol engineers say do not launch; management asks them to reconsider; the next morning Challenger breaks apart. What decides the investigation afterwards is nobody's memory — it is the RECORD: who said, who claimed, who assured NASA that it was safe, who acknowledged the risk and who denied ever hearing it. Listen to the Rogers Commission reading the minutes back: it is pure reported speech, and every reporting verb is worth a career.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and write down 8 reported-speech sentences from the investigation ("they told us that...", "he claimed that...", "management assured them that..."). For each one, decide: does the chosen verb protect the speaker, or expose him? Then rewrite THREE of them with a different reporting verb and feel the weight change.

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Big Dig (GBH) — Part 6: "The Up Down Charts" (~45 min, free)

The story of the largest urban infrastructure project in the United States — tunnels under Boston — and this episode is exactly your topic: for years the up-down charts shown in the meetings hid the real cost and schedule variance, until one disclosure pulls the curtain down. It is your whole lesson at the scale of billions: schedule slippage that nobody escalates, a steering committee kept in the dark, and the gap between what was said in the meetings and what the schedule actually showed. Native American English, real pace, journalistic storytelling.

Tip: listen ONCE without pausing. Then record 90 seconds, out loud, reporting the episode as if it were a set of minutes: what the managers claimed, what they never acknowledged, what the public was told and what the audit found. Use at least FOUR different reporting verbs.

Listen ↗
YouTube
ALL the REPORTING VERBS for B2 First (FCE) — SMASH English (13 min)

The exact structure of the lesson, at the exact level, from the same channel as the video in Lesson 6. Every reporting verb with its PATTERN: assure somebody THAT + clause · undertake / promise / offer TO + infinitive · deny / acknowledge + -ing · accuse somebody OF + -ing. It is the pattern, not the meaning, that trips up Brazilian speakers: "they assured us to send the schedule" is wrong; "they undertook to send it" and "they assured us that they would send it" are right.

Tip: after the video, take the last real meeting you had with a supplier and write 6 sentences reporting what each side said — one with assured us that, one with undertook to, one with claimed that, one with acknowledged, one with denied and one with told us that. Put the DATE in front of every one of them. That is the summary email you are actually going to send.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Say Exactly What You Mean -- Writing the RFI

Documentary
Downfall: The Case Against Boeing (Netflix, 89 min)

Your lesson at the scale of two disasters. The 737 MAX was certified as if it were the SAME aircraft as before — and the question you learned to ask today is the exact question nobody asked in time: the standard to which the design was certified, and the conditions under which it was tested. Watch what the documents did NOT say: MCAS existed, MCAS acted on its own, and MCAS was simply not in the manual. And watch how the company applies old acceptance criteria to a new design, and treats a design difference as if it were a minor variation. It is a documentary about ambiguity in technical documentation — and about who ends up paying for it.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and write down 8 sentences with a relative clause said by the engineers and the investigators ("the system that pushed the nose down", "the pilots, who had never been told about it, ..."). For EACH one, decide: does the clause DEFINE (say which ones) or ADD (talk about all of them)? Then rewrite THREE of them, moving the commas, and watch the meaning — and the liability — change owner.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Cautionary Tales (Tim Harford) — "Cleared For Take-Off? (Tenerife Air Disaster 1)" (~40 min, free)

The most expensive case of ambiguity in the history of engineering, and it fits into four words. In 1977, at Tenerife, a Dutch captain said over the radio "We are now at take-off" — and the tower understood that he was HOLDING at the take-off point, when he meant that he was TAKING OFF. 583 people died. Nobody got the English wrong: the sentence was grammatically perfect and it could, in good faith, be understood in two ways. It is the exact definition of what you saw today — and it happens between non-native speakers, under schedule pressure, in technical English: your world, with Germans, French and Chinese engineers on the same call.

Tip: listen ONCE without pausing. Then stop at the fatal sentence and write THREE versions of it that could NOT be read in two ways. Next, open the last technical email you sent to a supplier and find ONE sentence of your own that allows two readings. Rewrite it. That is the whole skill.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Defining and Non-Defining Relative Clauses — Essential B2 First (FCE) Grammar · SMASH English (12 min)

The exact structure of the lesson, at the exact level, from the same channel as the videos in lessons 6 and 7. The two clauses side by side, with the rule that decides everything: with no comma the clause DEFINES (it says which ones) and accepts that; with commas it ADDS (it talks about all of them) and that becomes impossible — only which. He also covers reduced relatives and the preposition in front of which, which is the register of the RFI. Watch it AFTER the lesson: the video will sound obvious, and that is the point.

Tip: after the video, open the last REAL RFI or technical note you wrote at Motiva and audit it: underline every relative clause and ask yourself "how many units did I just commit?". Then write 6 new sentences about your own project — two defining, two non-defining, one reduced, and one with preposition + which. Those six sentences are the RFI you are going to send for real.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Say That Again -- The Commissioning Call at Full Speed

Movie
Apollo 13 (1995, Ron Howard) — 140 min · available in Brazil (see where through the link)

The whole movie is your lesson: engineers listening to technical English, fast, over noise, with no right to get a date or a number wrong. Watch the PROTOCOL they use — the one you practiced today: nobody ever just says "ok". They READ BACK what they heard — "Say again?", "Copy that, two amps", "Just to confirm, we're powering down the LEM?" — and only then act. Watch, too, how mission control forecasts with a date attached, exactly the way Fiona does: "By the time they hit the atmosphere, we'll have burned the last of it". That whole room does, under maximum pressure, what you will be doing on your next call with Motiva.

Tip: watch it with ENGLISH subtitles and write down 10 moments where somebody confirms before acting. Then turn the subtitles off and watch the re-entry checklist scene again (from the 100-minute mark): how many numbers do you catch on the first listen? Finally, write THREE sentences of your own with will have + past participle and THREE with will be + -ing, about the commissioning schedule of Line 5 itself.

Where to watch ↗
Podcast
Freakonomics Radio — "Here's Why All Your Projects Are Always Late (and What to Do About It)" (~45 min, free)

Native English, real pace, your subject: why EVERY project runs late — and why the fault lies not with the supplier, but with the way people FORECAST the future.
