They explain the "planning fallacy": the human brain estimates the best-case deadline and forgets to factor in everything that always happens. In other words, the antidote is exactly today's grammar — you stop saying "we will finish" and start saying "by the twenty-third we will have closed every outstanding item", with a date somebody can hold you to. This is the most valuable listening input of the week: native speed, no subtitles, about your own job.

Tip: listen ONCE with no pauses and no transcript, at 1x (never 0.75x — that is the whole point of this lesson). Write down only the NUMBERS and the DATES you managed to catch. Then listen again with the transcript from the website open, and count how many you missed. Finally, write 5 sentences applying the "planning fallacy" to your own Line 5 schedule, using will have + past participle in all five.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Do YOU know the FUTURE CONTINUOUS?! — C1 Advanced English Grammar · SMASH English (12 min)

The grammar of this lesson at exactly your level, from the same channel as the videos in Lessons 6, 7 and 8. He puts will be + -ing side by side with will have + past participle and with the simple future, and shows why the choice changes what you are PROMISING — which is precisely what decides whether your commitment on the call is an intention or a date. Watch it AFTER the lesson: it will sound obvious, and that is the idea.

Tip: after the video, take the REAL schedule of your next three-week window at Motiva and write 8 sentences: four with will have + past participle (what will be DONE on each date) and four with will be + -ing (what will be HAPPENING on that day). Record them out loud, fast, running the auxiliary together ("we'll've closed'em by Friday") — producing the reduced form is what trains your ear to catch it on the next call.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — The Interview Room -- Performing Under Pressure

Film scene
The Pursuit of Happyness — the job interview scene (Sony, 4 min)

Chris Gardner walks into the most important interview of his life straight from a night in a police cell, in paint-stained clothes, completely unprepared. Watch what he does with it. He does not apologize his way out of the room and he does not sell himself short: he thinks on his feet, tells the truth in one honest line, and turns a disaster into the answer they remember. It is a masterclass in how you come across as the right person even when nothing is going your way — and it is exactly the composure you rehearsed tonight for your own panel.

Tip: watch once, then pause after his answer about the shirt. Write down, in English, the single sentence he uses to reframe the situation. Then write YOUR version for a real question you fear ("Why did the Brussels interview go badly?") — one honest sentence, no apology, that makes you come across as senior, not defensive.

Watch ↗
Podcast
B-Schooled — "The STAR method for essays and interview questions" (Episode 69, ~15 min, free)

Fifteen minutes on the exact tool you built tonight. The hosts break the STAR method down — Situation, Task, Action, Result — and, more usefully, they name the mistake you already know is yours: telling the Situation and stopping, so the Result never lands. Listen for how they answer a behavioral question out loud, and notice how much of the impact lives in the final sentence. This is the audio version of the artifact you filled in with the six-units story.

Tip: listen once. Then take your own supplier-conflict story from the lesson and record a 90-second STAR answer on your phone, out loud, in English. Play it back and check one thing only: is the Result there, with a number? If it is not, you have a story, not a STAR answer — fix it and record again.

Listen ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "'used to', 'be used to' and 'get used to'" (English Class, 8 min)

The exact grammar point of tonight, taught by native teachers at your level. They put the three structures side by side and drill the one thing that decides everything: the verb form. used to + base verb for the finished past, be / get used to + -ing for what is already normal — and why "I'm used to manage" is the mistake a hiring panel hears immediately. Watch it AFTER the lesson: it will sound obvious, and that is the point.

Tip: after the video, write six sentences about your own career — two with "used to" (where you came from), two with "be used to" (what is normal now), and two with "got used to" (what you adapted to). Read them aloud. Those six sentences are your "tell me about yourself" answer, already built.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Milestone Review -- Consolidating What You Know

Movie
Moneyball (2011, Bennett Miller) — 133 min · available in Brazil (see where through the link)

A film built out of exactly the two things this lesson trains: leverage and the counterfactual. Billy Beane runs a whole season on one insight nobody else will name — the elephant in the room — and every negotiation in the movie is a debrief under pressure: he owns his side, he anchors his position in the data he cannot be argued out of, and he refuses the shortcut everyone offers him. Listen for the conditionals, because the whole story is one long "if we had done it the old way, we wouldn't be here" — the mixed conditional you learned today, spoken by native professionals under real stakes. The trade-negotiation scenes are a masterclass in pushing back without burning the bridge.

Tip: watch with ENGLISH subtitles and write down 8 sentences where somebody uses a conditional to explain a decision. Mark which are pure third conditional (both halves in the past) and which are MIXED (past cause, present result). Then take one real fact from your Line 5 project and say it five ways — cleft, inversion, causative, future perfect, mixed conditional — out loud, the way the director in tonight's listening asked you to.

Where to watch ↗
Talk
William Ury — "The Walk from No to Yes" · TED (~19 min, free, subtitled)

The co-author of Getting to Yes on the exact move you practiced in the role-play: how to hold your position in a hard negotiation without turning it into a fight. His central idea — go to the "balcony," name the real problem, and give the other side a way to say yes without losing face — is the debrief with Camille in one talk. Native English, clean and measured pace, the register of a professional who never once raises his voice. Watch it and you will hear the difference between attacking a supplier and pushing back on one.

Tip: listen once with no subtitles and write down the three moves he says a negotiator has to make. Then listen again with English subtitles on. Finally, map each move onto tonight's call: where did João "go to the balcony," where did he name the elephant in the room, and where did he give Camille a way to say yes? Write one sentence for each, using a structure from the lesson.

Watch on TED ↗
YouTube
All Conditionals (0, 1, 2, 3 and MIXED) — English Grammar · Arnel's Everyday English (~30 min)

The one new structure of the lesson, taught inside the full family you already know. Arnel puts the mixed conditional side by side with the second and third conditionals, and shows exactly the trap tonight's Common Mistake slide warned about: when the result is NOW, you drop "would have" and say "would." Her examples are natural, adult and native-paced. Watch it AFTER the lesson — it will feel like confirmation of something you already used in the role-play, which is the whole point of a milestone review.

Tip: after the video, write 6 mixed conditionals about your real work at Motiva — three "past cause, present result" (If we had released the schedule on time, we wouldn't be...) and three "present truth, past result" (If I weren't so strict on compliance, I would have...). Record them out loud, then check each one against the two-mistake rule from the Grammar Tip: is the present-result half using "would + base," and is the present-truth condition using the past simple?

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Navigating Conflict -- Pushing Back Without Burning Bridges

Talk
William Ury — "The Walk from No to Yes" (TEDx, 18 min)

Ury wrote Getting to Yes and spent his career in the hardest conflicts on earth. In eighteen minutes he gives you the two moves you rehearsed tonight: go to the balcony before you react, and look for the common ground instead of the win. His whole thesis is that the point of a difficult conversation is not to defeat the other side but to reach an agreement both can live with — which is exactly what "pushing back without burning bridges" means when the supplier is blaming you for a delay.

Tip: watch once. Then take the GleisTech scenario and write, in English, one sentence for each of Ury's moves — one that "goes to the balcony" (defuses the heat) and one that names the common ground. Those two sentences are the opening of your next real conflict call.

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — "Learn to Disagree More Effectively" (Ep. 1071, ~26 min)

Harvard's Julia Minson has studied for years what people get wrong in disagreement. Her finding is the spine of tonight's lesson: the goal of a good disagreement is not to win — it is to keep the conversation going. She shows how "receptiveness" is built from specific language choices, the same concede-then-contrast move you drilled: acknowledge the other side's point out loud before you make your own. Listen for how often the strongest move is to give ground first.

Tip: as you listen, note three "receptive" phrases she models. Then rewrite your firmest line to GleisTech ("the post-approval slip is yours") so it opens with one of them — "I can see why you'd frame it that way; however..." Say the new version aloud until it sounds assertive, not aggressive.

Listen ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — "Linking words of contrast" (English Class, ~7 min)

The exact grammar of tonight, from native teachers at your level. They set the concession words side by side and drill the one thing that trips up every B2 speaker: what comes AFTER the connector. although / even though / while take a full clause; despite / in spite of take a noun or -ing — so "despite the delay", never "despite we were late". Watch it after the lesson: it will sound obvious, and that is the point.

Tip: after the video, write six sentences about the GleisTech dispute — two with "although", two with "despite" (+ noun), and two with "however" (between two sentences). Read them aloud. Those six sentences are the backbone of the email you will write for homework.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Reading the Fine Print -- When the Standard Moved and Nobody Told You

Documentary
Challenger: The Final Flight (Netflix, 2020) — 4 episodes · on Netflix in Brazil

This is your lesson at its most serious. The investigation board does exactly what you did today: it reconstructs the sequence, event by event, in the past perfect. The killer detail is one you will recognize — the engineers had flagged the O-ring seal, management had overruled them, and by the morning of the launch the warning had been sitting in a memo for months. Watch how the whole story turns on the ORDER of events: who had known what, and by when. That is the difference between a shared discrepancy and a breach — and it is decided entirely by the timeline.

Tip: watch Episode 4 (the investigation) with ENGLISH subtitles and write down 8 sentences in the past perfect — what each party had done, or had failed to do, before the launch. Then turn the subtitles off and listen for the reduced "had" ('d) in the interviews. Finally, write THREE sentences reconstructing your own axle-counter non-conformance the same way.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
99% Invisible — Episode 110, "Structural Integrity" (~30 min, free, plays in the browser)

Native English, real pace, your exact subject: an engineer discovers, AFTER a skyscraper is finished, that a flaw had been introduced during construction and nobody had caught it — a bolted joint that a change order had quietly replaced with a weaker one. What follows is a secret, night-by-night repair. Listen for how the story is told: it only makes sense in the past perfect, because everything that matters had already happened before anyone noticed. This is the fine print of a building, and it is the closest thing on a podcast to reading Annex D too late.

Tip: listen ONCE at 1x with no transcript and note only the ORDER of events — what had happened before the flaw was found. Then open the transcript on the page and count how many "had + participle" you missed on the first pass. Finally, write 5 sentences applying the same reconstruction to a standard revision at Motiva, using the past perfect in all five.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Most People Get This Wrong! Past Perfect Simple v Continuous (+ TEST!) (~15 min)

The grammar of this lesson, at exactly your level. It puts had + past participle next to had been + -ing and shows why the choice changes what you are saying — which is precisely what decides, in a non-conformance, whether you have proved the order of events or blurred it. Watch it AFTER the lesson: the "which came first" logic will already feel familiar, and the test at the end will tell you where your instinct still drops the "had".

Tip: after the video, take the real revision history of a technical standard you use at Motiva and write 8 sentences: four with had + past participle (what had been changed, and when) and four with had been + -ing (how long each revision had been in force). Record them out loud, fast, collapsing the "had" into a 'd — producing the reduced form is what trains your ear to catch it in a real dispute.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Making Your Case -- Presenting Technical Findings

Talk
Nancy Duarte — "The secret structure of great talks" (TEDxEast, ~18 min)

Duarte reverse-engineered hundreds of the best presentations ever given and found they all share one shape. It is the same move you drilled tonight: start where the audience is, show them where you want them to be, and make the gap between the two impossible to ignore. Watch it as a template for your own status update — the executive summary is your "what is", the recommendation is your "what could be", and the call to action is the leap you ask the client to take.

Tip: watch once, then map your Line 4 update onto her structure. Write the one sentence that is your "what is" (the twelve-week slip) and the one that is your "what could be" (recoverable to four). Those two sentences are your opening.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart (Stanford) — Ep. 193: "Conquer Questions" (~25 min)

Matt Abrahams from Stanford takes apart the exact moment you rehearsed tonight: the hard question you did not see coming. His finding is that Q&A feels dangerous only because we treat it as an interruption instead of the point. He gives a simple structure for answering under pressure — answer first, then one detail, then why it matters — and shows why naming a pause ("let me think about that for a moment") reads as control, not weakness. It is the spine of the firmware question you handled in the dialogue.

Tip: as you listen, write down his answer structure. Then take the two hardest questions a client could ask about your Line 4 delay and draft a three-part answer for each — answer, detail, why it matters. Say them aloud until the pause feels comfortable.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Victoria's Lingua Lounge — "Participle Clauses & Reduced Relative Clauses (B2/C1)" (~12 min)

The exact grammar of tonight, at your level. She drills the one decision that trips up every B2 writer: after you drop who / which / that + be, is it a past participle or an -ing form? Her test is the same one you learned — does the noun do the action or receive it — and she shows how these clauses are everywhere in formal, technical writing. Watch it after the lesson and the briefing note you read will suddenly look transparent.

Tip: after the video, rewrite three sentences from your Line 4 briefing as full relative clauses, then reduce them back — "the equipment which was delivered late" → "the equipment delivered late". Producing them by hand is what moves the grammar from recognised to owned.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Speaking Under Pressure -- Handling Tough Questions in Real Time

TED Talk
Sian Leah Beilock -- "Why we choke under pressure and how to avoid it" (TED, ~13 min, free, plays in the browser)

This is the science behind your entire lesson. Beilock is a cognitive scientist who studies exactly what happened to you in the March interview: under pressure, the brain's working memory — the space you think in — gets eaten by worry, so the skill you have suddenly will not come out. She explains why, and gives concrete ways to stop it: rehearse under mild stress, focus outward instead of on yourself, and reframe the pressure. It is the mechanism under every phrase you drilled tonight: buy time, reframe, keep your cool.

Tip: watch once with ENGLISH subtitles and note the two or three techniques she names for not choking. Then write, in your own words, how each one applies to the Élise interview — what you will do the moment a question lands on the spot. Record a 90-second summary of the talk, out loud, as if briefing a colleague who freezes in interviews.

Watch on TED ↗
Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart (Stanford GSB) -- Ep. 107, "How to Speak Successfully When You're Put on the Spot" (~25 min, free on YouTube, plays in the browser)

Matt Abrahams teaches strategic communication at Stanford, and this episode is your lesson from the inside: how to answer well when you are put on the spot with no time to prepare. He gives the exact moves you practiced — slow down, buy yourself time, ask a clarifying question, reframe the challenge — and explains why trying to be "perfect" is what makes you freeze. Native English, real pace, and everything transfers straight into an interview or a supplier call.

Tip: listen once and write down every specific technique he gives for handling a question on the spot. Then take the three pressure questions from tonight's screening card and script your opening move for each — the phrase you use to buy time before you answer. Record all three, and make sure at least one answer uses would rather, had better or may as well.

Listen on YouTube ↗
YouTube
Would Rather vs Had Better -- the difference between WOULD RATHER and HAD BETTER (~10 min)

The exact grammar of tonight's lesson, at your level, from a clear native teacher. It puts would rather and had better side by side (and touches may as well), and shows why each one changes the tone of what you say — preference versus warning — which is precisely what keeps you sounding senior instead of cornered under pressure. Watch it AFTER the lesson to fix the two errors Brazilians make most: adding "to" after the modal, and saying "would better" instead of "had better".

Tip: after the video, write 9 sentences from your real work at Motiva -- three with would rather (a preference in a negotiation), three with had better (advice to a supplier), three with may as well (accepting a fixed constraint). Record them fast, collapsing would/had into a 'd, with a bare verb right after. Then say each one as if Élise had just pushed you on it.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Holding Your Ground -- Defending Technical Decisions Under Pressure

Talk
Chris Voss — "Never Split the Difference" (TEDxUniversityofNevada, ~17 min)

A former FBI lead hostage negotiator explains the exact skill you drilled tonight: how to stay firm on your position while staying warm on the person across the table. His "tactical empathy" is what turns "I understand the concern, and I've taken it seriously" from a soft phrase into a tool — you acknowledge the other side fully, and it lowers the temperature without conceding an inch. Watch it as the mindset behind holding your ground: the hostile claim is information about what they want, not an attack on you.

Tip: as you watch, write down two of his phrases for acknowledging the other side. Then rewrite your opening to Carmen using them — acknowledge the concern, then dispute the claim with the record.

Watch on TED ↗
Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart (Stanford) — Ep. 250: "How to Navigate Conflict" (~30 min)

Stanford's Matt Abrahams takes apart the moment you rehearsed tonight: the conversation where the other side thinks the problem is yours. The through-line is exactly your skill — what makes a dispute hard is rarely the topic, it is the inner reaction each person is having. The episode gives concrete moves for staying productive under that pressure: acknowledge before you argue, separate the position from the person, and keep the relationship intact while you hold your line.

Tip: pick one tool from the episode and script it into your Ibersign meeting. Draft the two sentences you would say to acknowledge Carmen's concern before you put the sign-off log on the table.

Listen ↗
Grammar
Cambridge Dictionary — "The subjunctive" (English Grammar Today, ~10 min read)

The exact grammar of tonight, at your level, from a reference you can trust. It lays out the one move that made your claim letter sound authoritative: after essential / important / recommend / insist / request that, the verb stays in the base form for every subject — "It is essential that he submit", "We recommend that the team be notified". It also covers the negative ("that he not be present") and shows why formal English prefers this to "should". Read it after the lesson and the corrective-action letter will look transparent.

Tip: after reading, take three "should / needs to" sentences from a real Motiva document and rewrite each as a subjunctive requirement — "the supplier should submit" → "it is essential that the supplier submit". Producing them by hand is what moves the grammar from recognised to owned.

Read on Cambridge ↗
